Haue a care o'your fine veluet caps, Little-wit is gazing at the ſigne; which is the Pigs-head with a large writing vnder it. the Fayre is duſty. Take a ſweet delicate Booth, with boughs, here, i'the way, and coole your ſelues i'the ſhade: you and your friends. The beſt pig and bottle-ale i' the Fayre, Sir. Old Vrſla is Cooke, there you may read: the pigges head ſpeakes it. Poore ſoule, ſhee has had a Sringhalt, the Maryhinchco: but ſhee's prettily amended.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: A delicate ſhow-pig, little Miſtris, with ſhweet ſauce, and crackling, like de bay-leafe i'de fire, la! Tou ſhalt ha'de cleane ſide o'de table-clot and di glaſſ vaſh'd with phaterſh of Dame Annesſh Cleare.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: This's ſine, verily, here be the beſt pigs: and ſhee doe's roaſt 'hem as well as euer ſhe did; the Pigs head ſayes.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Excellent, excellent, Miſtris, with fire o' Iuniper and Roſe-mary branches! The Oracle of the Pigs head, that, Sir.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Sonne, were you not warn'd of the vanity of the eye? haue you forgot the wholeſome admonition, ſo ſoone?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Good mother, how ſhall we finde a pigge, if we doe not looke about for't? will it run off o'the ſpit, into our mouths thinke you? as in Lubberland? and cry, we, we?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: No, but your mother, religiouſly wiſe, conceiueth it may offer it ſelfe, by other meanes, to the ſenſe, as by way of ſteeme, which I thinke it doth, Buſy ſents after it like a Hound. here in this place (Huh, huh) yes, it doth. and it were a ſinne of obſtinacy, great obſtinacy, high and horrible obſtinacy, to decline, or reſiſt the good titillation of the famelick ſenſe, which is the ſmell. Therefore be bold (huh, huh, huh) follow the ſent. Enter the Tents of the vncleane, for once, and ſatisfie your wiues frailty. Let your fraile wife be ſatisfied: your zealous mother, and my ſuffering ſelfe, will alſo be ſatisfied.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Come, Win, as good winny here, as goe farther, and ſee nothing.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Wee ſcape ſo much of the other vanities, by our earely entring.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: It is an aedifying conſideration.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: This is ſcuruy, that wee muſt come into the Fayre, and not looke on't.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Win, haue patience, Win, I'le tell you more anon.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Moone-calfe; entertaine within there, the beſt pig i'the Booth; a Porklike pig. Theſe are Banbury-bloods, o'the ſincere ſtud, come a pigge-hunting. Whit, wait Whit, looke to your charge.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: A pigge prepare, preſently, let a pigge be prepared to vs.

sp.speaker: MOO.
sp.p: S'light, who be theſe?

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Is this the good ſeruice, Iordan, you'ld doe me?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Why, Vrs? why, Vrs? thou'lt ha' vapours i'thy legge againe preſently, pray thee go in, 't may turne to the ſcratches elſe.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Hang your vapours, they are ſtale, and ſtinke like you, are theſe the gueſts o'the game, you promis'd to fill my pit withall, to day?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: I • what aile they Vrs?

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Aile they? they are all ſippers, ſippers o'the City, they looke as they would not drinke off two penn'orth of bottle-ale amongſt 'hem.

sp.speaker: MOO.
sp.p: A body may read that i'their ſmall printed ruffes.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Away, thou art a foole, Vrs, and thy Moone-calfe too, i'your ignorant vapours, now? hence, good gueſts, I ſay right hypocrites, good gluttons. In, and ſet a couple o'pigs o'the board, and halfe a dozen of the biggeſt bottles afore 'hem, and call Whit, I doe not loue to heare Innocents abus'd: Fine ambling hypocrites! and a ſtone-puritane, with a ſorrell head, and beard, good mouth'd gluttons: two to a pigge, away.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Are you ſure they are ſuch?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: O'the right breed, thou ſhalt try 'hem by the teeth, Vrs, where's this Whit?

l.hi: Behold, man and ſee, what a worthy man am ee!
With the fury of my ſword, and the ſhaking of my beard,
I will make ten thouſand men afeard.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Well ſaid, braue Whit, in, and feare the ale out o'the bottles, into the bellies of the brethren, and the ſiſters drinke to the cauſe, and pure vapours.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: My Roarer is turn'd Tapſter, mee thinks. Now were a fine time for thee, Win-wife, to lay aboard thy widdow, thou'lt neuer be Maſter of a better ſeaſon, or place; ſhee that will venture her ſelfe into the Fayre, and a pig-boxe, will admit any aſſault, be aſſur'd of that.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: I loue not enterpriſes of that ſuddenneſſe, though.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: I'le warrant thee, then, no wife out o'the widdowes Hundred: if I had but as much Title to her, as to haue breath'd once on that ſtreight ſtomacher of hers, I would now aſſure my felfe to carrry her, yet, ere ſhewent out of Smithfield. Or ſhe ſhould carry me, which were the fitter ſight, I confeſſe. But you are a modeſt vndertaker, by circumſtances, and degrees; come, 'tis Diſeaſe in thee, not Iudgement, I ſhould offer at all together. Looke, here's the poore foole, againe, that was ſtung by the waſpe, ere while.

I will make no more orations, ſhall draw on theſe tragicall concluſions. And I begin now to thinke, that by a ſpice of collaterall Iuſtice, Adam Ouerdoo, deſeru'd this beating; for I the ſaid Adam, was one cauſe (a by-cauſe) why the purſe was loſt: and my wiues brothers purſe too, which they know not of yet. But I ſhall make very good mirth with it, at ſupper, (that will be the ſport) and put my little friend, Mr Humphrey Waſp's choler quite out of countenance. When, ſitting at the vpper end o'my Table, as I vſe, & drinking to my brother Cokes, and Mrs. Alice Ouerdoo, as I wil, my wife, for their good affectiō to old Bradley, I deliuer to'hem, it was I, that was cudgell'd, and ſhew 'hem the marks. To ſee what bad euents may peepe out o'the taile of good purpoſes! the care I had of that ciuil yong man, I tooke fancy to this morning, (and haue not left it yet) drew me to that exhortation, which drew the company, indeeede, which drew the cut-purſe; which drew the money; which drew my brother Cokes his loſſe; which drew on Waſp's anger; which drew on my beating: a pretty gradation! And they ſhall ha' it i'their diſh, i'faith, at night for fruit: I loue to be merry at my Table. I had thought once, at one ſpeciall blow he ga'me, to haue reuealed my ſelfe? but then (I thank thee fortitude) I remembred that a wiſe man (and who is euer ſo great a part, o'the Commonwealth in himſelfe) for no particular diſaſter ought to abandon a publike good deſigne. The husbandman ought not for one vnthankful yeer, to forſake the plough; The Shepheard ought not, for one ſcabb'd ſheep, to throw by his tar-boxe; The Pilot ought not for one leake i'the poope, to quit the Helme; Nor the Alderman ought not for one cuſterd more, at a meale, to giue vp his cloake; The Conſtable ought not to breake his ſtaffe, and forſweare the watch, for one roaring night; Nor the Piper o'the Pariſh (Vt paruis componere magna ſolebam) to put vp his pipes, for one rainy Sunday. Theſe are certaine knocking concluſions; out of which, I am reſolu'd, come what come can, come beating, come impriſonment, come infamy, come baniſhment, nay, come the rack, come the hurdle, (welcome all) I will not diſcouer who I am, till my due time; and yet ſtill, all ſhall be, as I ſaid euer, in Iuſtice name, and the King's, and for the Common-wealth.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: What doe's he talke to himſelfe, and act ſo ſeriouſly? poore foole!

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: No matter what. Here's freſher argument, intend that.

div.head: ACT. III.
SCENE. IIIJ.
COKES. LEATHERHEAD. VVASPE. Miſtreſſe OVER DOO. WIN-VVIFE. QVARLOVS. TRASH. GRACE.

sp.p: COme, Miſtreſſe Grace, come Siſter, heere's more fine ſights, yet i'faith. Gods'lid where's Numps?

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: What doe you lacke, Gentlemen? what is't you buy? fine Rattles! Drummes? Babies? little Dogges? and Birds for Ladies? What doe you lacke?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Good honeſt Numpes, keepe afore, I am ſo afraid thou'lt loſe ſomewhat: my heart was at my mouth, when I miſt thee.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: You were beſt buy a whip i'your hand to driue me.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Nay, doe not miſtake, Numps, thou art ſo apt to miſtake: I would but watch the goods. Looke you now, the treble fiddle, was e'en almoſt like to be loſt.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Pray you take heede you loſe not your ſelfe: your beſt way, were e'en get vp, and ride for more ſurety. Buy a tokens worth of great pinnes, to faſten your ſelfe to my ſhoulder.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: What doe you lacke, Gentlemen? fine purſes, pouches, pincaſes, pipes? What is't you lacke? a paire o'ſmithes to wake you i'the morning? or a fine whiſtling bird?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Numps, here be finer things then any we ha' bought by oddes! and more delicate horſes, a great deale! good Numpes, ſtay, and come hither.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Will you ſcourſe with him? you are in Smithfield, you may fit your ſelfe with a fine eaſy-going ſtreet-nag, for your ſaddle again' Michaelmaſſe-terme, doe, has he ne'er a little odde cart for you, to make a Carroch on, i'the countrey, with foure pyed hobby horſes? why the meazills, ſhould you ſtand heere, with your traine, cheaping of Dogges, Birds, and Babies? you ha' no children to beſtow 'hem on? ha' you?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: No, but again' I ha' children, Numps, that's all one.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Do, do, do, do; how many ſhall you haue, think you? an' I were as you, I'ld buy for all my Tenants, too, they are a kind o'ciuill Sauages, that wil part with their children for rattles, pipes, and kniues. You were beſt buy a hatchet, or two, & truck with 'hem.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Good Numps, hold that little tongue o'thine, and faue it a labour. I am reſolute Bat, thou know'ft.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: A reſolute foole, you are, I know, and a very ſufficient Coxcombe; with all my heart; nay you haue it, Sir, and you be angry, turd i'your teeth, twice: (if I ſaid it not once afore) and much good doe you.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Was there euer ſuch a ſelfe-affliction? and ſo impertinent?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Alas! his care will goe neere to cracke him, let's in, and comfort him.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Would I had beene ſet i'the ground, all but the head on me, and had my braines bowl'd at, or threſh'd out, when firſt I vnderwent this plague of a charge!

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: How now, Numps! almoſt tir'd i'your Protectorſhip? ouerparted? ouerparted?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why, I cannot tell, Sir, it may be I am, dos't grieue you?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: No, I ſweare dos't not, Numps: to ſatisfie you.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Numps? S'blood, you are fine and familiar! how long ha' wee bin acquainted, I pray you?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: I thinke it may be remembred, Numps, that? 'twas ſince morning ſure.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why, I hope I know't well enough, Sir, I did not aske to be told.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: No? why then?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: It's no matter why, you ſee with your eyes, now, what I ſaid to you to day? you'll beleeue me another time?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Are you remouing the Fayre, Numps?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: A pretty queſtion! and a very ciuill one! yes faith, I ha' my lading you ſee; or ſhall haue anon, you may know whoſe beaſt I am, by my burthen. If the pannier-mans Iacke were euer better knowne by his loynes of mutton, I'le be flead, and feede dogs for him, when his time comes.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: How melancholi' Miſtreſſe Grace is yonder! pray thee let's goe enter our ſelues in Grace, with her.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Thoſe ſixe horſes, friend I'le haue—

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: How!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: And the three Iewes trumps; and halfe a dozen o'Birds, and that Drum, (I haue one Drumme already) and your Smiths; I like that deuice o'your ſmiths, very pretty well, and foure Halberts —and (le'me ſee) that fine painted great Lady, and her three women for ſtate, I'le haue.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: No, the ſhop; buy the whole ſhop, it will be beſt, the ſhop, the ſhop!

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: If his worſhip pleaſe.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Yes, and keepe it during the Fayre, Bobchin.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p:
Peace, Numps, friend, doe not meddle with him, an'
you be wiſe, and would ſhew your head aboue board: hee will ſting thorow your wrought night-cap, beleeue me. A ſet of theſe Violines, I would buy too, for a delicate young noiſe I haue i'the countrey, that are euery one a ſize leſſe then another, iuſt like your fiddles. I would faine haue a fine young Maſque at my marriage, now I thinke on't: but I doe want ſuch a number o'things. And Numps will not helpe me now, and I dare not ſpeake to him.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Will your worſhip buy any ginger-bread, very good bread, comfortable bread?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Ginger-bread! yes, let's ſee. He runnes to her ſhop.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: There's the tother ſprindge?

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Is this well, goody Ione? to interrupt my market? in the midſt? and call away my cuſtomers? can you anſwer this, at the Piepouldres?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Why? if his Maſter-ſhip haue a minde to buy, I hope my ware lies as open as another's; I may ſhew my ware, as well as you yours.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Hold your peace; I'le content you both: I'le buy vp his ſhop, and thy basket.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Will you i'faith?

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Why ſhould you put him from it, friend?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Cry you mercy! you'ld be ſold too, would you? what's the price on you? Ierkin, and all as you ſtand? ha' you any qualities?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Yes, good-man angry-man, you ſhall finde he has qualities, if you cheapen him.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Gods ſo, you ha' the ſelling of him! what are they? will they be bought for loue, or money?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: No indeed, Sir.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: For what then? victualls?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: He ſcornes victuals, Sir, he has bread and butter at home, thanks be to God! and yet he will do more for a good meale, if the toy take him i'the belly, mary then they muſt not ſet him at lower end; if they do, he'll goe away, though he faſt. But put him a top o'the Table, where his place is, and hee'll doe you forty fine things. Hee has not been ſent for, and ſought out for nothing, at your great citty-ſuppers, to put downe Coriat, and Cokeley, and bin laught at for his labour; he'll play you all the Puppets i'the towne ouer, and the Players, euery company, and his owne company too; he ſpares no body!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I'faith?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Hee was the firſt, Sir, that euer baited the fellow i'the beare's skin, an't like your worſhip: no dog euer came neer him, ſince. And for fine motions!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Is hee good at thoſe too? can hee ſet out a Maſque trow?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p:
O Lord, Maſter! ſought to farre, and neere, for his inuentions:
and hee engroſſes all, hee makes all the Puppets i'the Fayre.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Do'ſt thou (in noth) old veluet Ierkin? giue mee thy hand.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Nay, Sir, you ſhall ſee him in his veluet Ierkin, and a ſcarfe, too, at night, when you heare him interpret Maſter Little-wit's Motion.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Speake no more, but ſhut vp ſhop preſently, friend. I'le buy both it, and thee too, to carry downe with me, and her hamper, beſide. Thy ſhop ſhall furniſh out the Maſque, and hers the Banquet: I cannot goe leſſe, to ſet out any thing with credit. what's the price, at a word, o'thy whole ſhop, caſe, and all as it ſtands?

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Sir, it ſtands me in ſixe and twenty ſhillings ſeuen pence, halfe-peny, beſides three ſhillings for my ground.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well, thirty ſhillings will doe all, then! And what comes yours too?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Foure ſhillings, and eleauen pence, Sir, ground, and all, an't like your worſhip.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Yes, it do's like my worſhip very well, poore woman, that's fiue ſhillings more, what a Maſque ſhall I furniſh out, for forty ſhillings? (twenty pound ſcotſh) and a Banquet of Ginger-bread? there's a ſtately thing! Numps? Siſter? and my wedding gloues too? (that I neuer thought on afore.) All my wedding gloues, Ginger-bread? O me! what a deuice will there be? to make 'hem eate their fingers ends! and delicate Brooches for the Bride-men! and all! and then I'le ha' this poeſie put to 'hem: For the beſt grace, meaning Miſtreſſe Grace, my wedding poeſie.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: I am beholden to you, Sir, and to your Bartholmew-wit.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: You doe not meane this, doe you? is this your firſt purchaſe?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Yes faith, and I doe not thinke, Numpes, but thou'lt ſay, it was the wiſeſt Act, that euer I did in my wardſhip.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Like inough! I ſhall ſay any thing, I!

I Cannot beget a Proiect, with all my politicall braine, yet; my Proiect is how to fetch off this proper young man, from his debaucht company: I haue followed him all the Fayre ouer, and ſtill I finde him with this ſongſter: And I begin ſhrewdly to ſuſpect their familiarity; and the young man of a terrible taint, Poetry!
•• ith which idle diſeaſe, if he be infected, there's no hope of him, in a ſtate-courſe. Actum eſt, of him for a common-wealths-man: if hee goe to't in Rime, once.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Yonder he is buying o'Ginger-bread: ſet in quickly, before he part with too much on his money.

p.hi: My maſters and friends, and good people, draw neere, &c.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Ballads! harke, harke! pray thee, fellow, ſtay a little, He ruun's to the Ballad man. good Numpes, looke to the goods. What Ballads haſt thou? let me ſee, let me ſee my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why ſo! hee's flowne'to another lime-buſh, there he will flutter as long more; till hee ha' ne'r a feather left. Is there a vexation like this, Gentlemen? will you beleeue mee now, hereafter? ſhall I haue credit with you?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Yes faith, ſhalt thou, Numps, and thou art worthy on't, for thou ſweateſt for't. I neuer ſaw a young Pimpe errant, and his Squire better match'd.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Faith, the ſiſter comes after 'hem, well, too.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Nay, if you ſaw the Iuſtice her husband, my Guardian, you were fitted for the Meſſe, hee is ſuch a wiſe one his way—

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: I wonder, wee ſee him not heere.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: O! hee is too ſerious for this place, and yet better ſport then then the other three, I aſſure you, Gentlemen: where ere he is, though't be o'the Bench.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: How doſt thou call it! A caueat againſt cutpurſes! a good ieſt, i'faith, I would faine ſee that Daemon, your Cutpurſe, He ſhow's his purſe boaſtingly. you talke of, that delicate handed Diuell; they ſay he walkes hereabout; I would ſee him walke, now. Looke you ſiſter, here, here, let him come, ſiſter, and welcome. Ballad-man, do's any cutpurſes haunt hereabout? pray thee raiſe me one or two: beginne and ſhew me one.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p:
Sir, this is a ſpell againſt 'hem, ſpicke and ſpan new; and 'tis made as 'twere in mine owne perſon, and I ſing it in mine owne
〈1 page duplicate〉
〈1 page duplicate〉
defence. But 'twill coſt a penny alone, if you buy it.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: No matter for the price, thou doſt not know me, I ſee, I am an odd Bartholmew.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: Ha'ſt a fine picture, Brother?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: O Siſter, doe you remember the ballads ouer the Nurſery-chimney at home o' my owne paſting vp, there be braue pictures. Other manner of pictures, than theſe, friend.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Yet theſe will ſerue to picke the pictures out o' your pockets, you ſhall ſee.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: So, I heard 'hem ſay. Pray thee-mind him not, fellow: hee'll haue an oare in euery thing.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: It was intended Sir, as if a purſe ſhould chance to be cut in my preſence, now, I may be blameleſſe, though: as by the ſequell, will more plainely appeare.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: We ſhall find that i'the matter. Pray thee begin.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: To the tune of Paggingtons Pound, Sir.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Fa, la la la, la la la, fa la la la. Nay, I'll put thee in tune, and all! mine owne country dance! Pray thee begin.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: It is a gentle admonition, you muſt know, Sir, both to the purſe-cutter, and the purſe-bearer.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Not a word more, out o'the tune, an' thou lou'ſt mee: Fa, la la la, la la la, fa la la la. Come, when?

l.hi: My maſters and friends, and good people draw neere,
And looke to your purſes, for that I doe ſay;

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Ha, ha, this chimes! good counſell at firſt daſh.

l.hi: And though little money, in them you doe beare.
It coſt more to get, then to loſe in a day.
You oft haue beene told,
Both the young and the old;
And bidden beware of the cutpurſe ſo bold:
Then if you take heed not, free me from the curſe,
Who both giue you warning, for and, the cutpurſe.
Youth, youth, thou hadſt better bin ſtaru'd by thy Nurſe,
Then liue to be hanged for cutting a purſe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Good!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well ſaid! hee were to blame that wold not i'faith.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Good i'faith, how ſay you, Numps? Is there any harme i'this?

l.hi: It hath bin vpbrayded to men of my trade,
That oftē times we are the cauſe of this crime.
Alacke and for pitty, why ſhould it be ſaid?
As if they regarded or places, or time.
Examples haue been
Of ſome that were ſeen,
In Weſtminſter Hall, yea the pleaders between,
Then why ſhould the Iudges be free from this curſe,
More then my poore ſelfe, for cutting the purſe?
Youth, youth, thou hadſt better bin ſtaru'd by thy Nurſe,
Then liue to be hanged for cutting a purſe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: The more coxcōbes they that did it, I wuſſe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: God a mercy for that! why ſhould they be more free indeede?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: That againe, good Ballad-man, that againe. O rare!He ſings the burden with him. I would faine rubbe mine elbow now, but I dare not pull out my hand. On, I pray thee, hee that made this ballad, ſhall be Poet to my Maſque.

sp.speaker: NIG.

l.hi: At Worc'ter'tis knowne well, and euen i'the layle,
A Knight of good worſhip did there ſhew his face,
Againſt the foule ſinners, in zealè for to rayle,

sp.l: And loſt (ipſo facto) his purſe in the place.

l.hi: Nay, once from the Seat
Of Iudgement ſo great,
A Iudge there did loſe a faire pouch of veluete.
O Lord for thy mercy, how wicked or worſe,
Are thoſe that ſo venture their necks for a purſe! Youth, youth, &c.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Is it poſſible?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I'faith?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Youth, youth, &c? pray thee ſtay a little, friend, yet o'thy conſcience, Numps, ſpeake, is there any harme i'this?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: To tell you true, 'tis too good for you, leſſe you had grace to follow it.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: It doth diſcouer enormitie, I'le marke it more: I ha' not lik'd a paltry piece of poetry, ſo well a good while.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Youth, youth, &c! where's this youth, now? A man muſt call vpon him, for his owne good, and yet hee will not appeare: looke here, here's for him; handy-dandy, Hee ſhewes his purſe. which hand will he haue? On, I pray thee, with the reſt, I doe heare of him, but I cannot ſee him, this Maſter Youth, the cutpurſe.

sp.speaker: NIG.

l.hi: At Playes and at Sermons, and at the Seſſions,
'Tis daily their practice ſuch booty to make:
Yea, vnder the Gallowes, at Executions,

sp.l: They ſticke not the Stare-abouts purſes to take.

l.hi: Nay one without grace,
at a better place,

sp.l: At Court, & in Chriſtmas, before the Kings face,

l.hi: Alacke then for pitty muſt I beare the curſe,
That onely belongs to the cunning cutpurſe?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: That was a fine fellow! I would haue him, now.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: But where's their cunning, now, when they ſhould vſe it? they are all chain'd now, I warrant you. Youth, youth, thou hadſt better, &c. The Rat-catchers charme, are all fooles and Aſſes to this! A poxe on 'hem, that they will not come! that a man ſhould haue ſuch a deſire to a thing, and want it.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: 'Fore God, I'ld giue halfe the Fayre, and 'twere mine, for a cutpurſe for him, to ſaue his longing. Hee ſhewes his purſe againe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Looke you Siſter, heere, heere, where is't now? which pocket is't in? for a wager?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I beſeech you leaue your wagers, and let him end his matter, an't may be.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: O, are you aedified Numps?

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Indeed hee do's interrupt him, too much: There Numps ſpoke to purpoſe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: againe. Siſter, I am an Aſſe, I cannot keepe my purſe: on, on; I pray thee, friend.

l.hi: But O, you vile nation of cutpurſes all,
Relent and repent,
l.stage: Edgworth gets vp to him, and • i •• es him in the eare with a ſtraw twice to draw his hand out of his pocket.
l.hi: and amend and be ſound,
And know that you ought not, by honeſt mens fall,
Aduance your owne fortunes, to die aboue ground,
And though you goe gay,
In ſilkes as you may,
It is not the high way to heauen, (as they ſay)
Repent then, repent you, for better, for worſe:
And kiſſe not the Gallowes for cutting a purſe.
Youth, youth, thou hadſt better bin ſteru'd by thy Nurſe,
Then liue to be hanged for cutting a purſe.

sp.speaker: WIN W.
sp.p: Will you ſee ſport? looke, there's a fellow gathers vp to him, marke.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Good, 'i faith • ome has lighted on the wrōg pocket.

sp.speaker: WIN W.
sp.p: He has it, 'fore God hee is a braue fellow; pitty hee ſhould be detected.

sp.speaker: ALL
sp.p: An excellent ballad! an excellent ballad!

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Friend, let mee ha' the firſt, let mee ha' the firſt, I pray you.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Pardon mee, Sir. Firſt come, firſt ſeru'd; and I'le buy the whole bundle too.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: That conueyance was better then all, did you ſee't? he has giuen the purſe to the ballad-ſinger.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Has hee?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Sir, I cry you mercy; I'le not hinder the poore mans profit: pray you miſtake me not.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Sir, I take you for an honeſt Gentleman; if that be miſtaking, I met you to day afore: ha! humh! O God! my purſe is gone, my purſe, my purſe, &c.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Come, doe not make a ſtirre, and cry your ſelfe an Aſſe, thorow the Fayre afore your time.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Why, haſt thou it, Numpes? good Numpes, how came you by it? I mar'le!

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I pray you ſeeke fome other gamſter, to play the foole with: you may loſe it time enough, for all your Fayre-wit.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: By this good hand, gloue and all, I ha' loſt it already, if thou haſt it not: feele elſe, and Miſtris Grace's handkercher, too, out o'the tother pocket.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why, 'tis well; very well, exceeding pretty, and well.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Are you ſure you ha' loſt it, Sir?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: O God! yes; as I am an honeſt man, I had it but e'en now, at youth, youth.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: I hope you ſuſpect not me, Sir.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Thee? that were a ieſt indeede! Doſt thou thinke the Gentleman is fooliſh? where hadſt thou hands, I pray thee? Away Aſſe, away.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I ſhall be beaten againe, if I be ſpi'd.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Sir, I ſuſpect an odde fellow, yonder, is ſtealing away.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: Brother, it is the preaching fellow! you ſhall ſuſpect him. He was at your tother purſe, you know! Nay, ſtay, Sir, and view the worke you ha' done, an' you be benefic'd at the Gallowes, and preach there, thanke your owne handy-worke.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Sir, you ſhall take no pride in your preferment: you ſhall be ſilenc'd quickly.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: What doe you meane? ſweet buds of gentility.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: To ha' my peneworths out on you: Bud. No leſſe then two purſes a day, ſerue you? I thought you a ſimple fellow, when my man Numpes beate you, i'the morning, and pittied you—

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: So did I, I'll beſworne, brother; but now I ſee hee is a lewd, and pernicious Enormity: (as Maſter Ouerdoo calls him.)

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Mine owne words turn'd vpon mee, like ſwords.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Cannot a man's purſe be at quiet for you, i'the Maſters pocket, but you muſt intice it forth, and debauch it?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Sir, Sir, keepe your debauch, and your fine Bartholmew-termes to your ſelfe; and make as much on'hem as you pleaſe. But gi'me this from you, i'the meane time: I beſeech you, ſee if I can looke to this. Waſp takes the Licence from him.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Why, Numps?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why? becauſe you are an Aſſe, Sir, there's a reaſon the ſhorteſt way, and you will needs ha' it; now you ha' got the tricke of loſing, you'ld loſe your breech, an't 'twere loofe. I know you, Sir, come, deliuer, you'll goe and cracke the vermine, you breed now, will you? 'tis very fine, will you ha' the truth on't? they are ſuch retchleſſe flies as you are, that blow cutpurſes abroad in euery corner; your fooliſh hauing of money, makes 'hem. An' there were no wiſer then I, Sir, the trade ſhoud lye open for you, Sir, it ſhould i'faith, Sir. I would teach your wit to come to your head, Sir, as well as your land to come into your hand, I aſſure you, Sir.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Alacke, good Numps.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nay, Gentlemen, neuer pitty mee, I am not worth it: Lord ſend me at home once, to Harrow o'the Hill againe, if I trauell any more, call me Coriat; withall my heart.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Stay, Sir, I muſt haue a word with you in priuate. Doe you heare?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: With me, Sir? what's your pleaſure? good Sir.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Doe not deny it. You are a cutpurſe, Sir, this Gentleman here, and I, ſaw you, nor doe we meane to detect you (though we can ſufficiently informe our ſelues, toward the danger of concealing you) but you muſt doe vs a piece of ſeruice.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Good Gentlemen; doe not vndoe me; I am a ciuill young man, and but a beginner, indeed.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p:
Sir, your beginning ſhall bring on your ending, for vs.
We are no Catchpoles nor Conſtables. That you are to vndertake, is this; you ſaw the old fellow, with the blacke boxe, here?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: The little old Gouernour, Sir?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: That ſame: I ſee, you haue flowne him to a marke already. I would ha' you get away that boxe from him, and bring it vs.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Would you ha' the boxe and all, Sir? or onely that, that is in't? I'le get you that, and leaue him the boxe, to play with ſtill: (which will be the harder o'the two) becauſe I would gaine your worſhips good opinion of me.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: He ſayes well, 'tis the greater Maſtry, and 'twill make the more ſport when 'tis miſt.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: I, and 'twill be the longer a miſſing, to draw on the ſport.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: But looke you doe it now, ſirrah, and keepe your word: or—

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Sir, if euer I breake my word, with a Gentleman, may I neuer read word at my need. Where ſhall I find you?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Some-where i'the Fayre, heereabouts. Diſpatch it quickly. I would faine ſee the carefull foole deluded! of all Beaſts, I loue the ſerious Aſſe. He that takes paines to be one, and playes the foole, with the greateſt diligence that can be.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Then you would not choſe, Sir, but loue my Guardian, Iuſtice Ouerdoo, who is anſwerable to that deſcription, in euery haire of him.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: So I haue heard. But how came you, Miſtis Welborne, to be his Ward? or haue relation to him, at firſt?

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Faith, through a common calamity, he bought me, Sir; and now he will marry me to his wiues brother, this wiſe Gentleman, that you ſee, or elſe I muſt pay value o'my land

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: S'lid, is there no deuice of diſparagement? or ſo? talke with ſome crafty fellow, ſome pick locke o'the Law! Would I had ſtudied a yeere longer i'the Innes of Court, and't had beene but i'your caſe.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: I Maſter Quarlous, are you proffering?

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: You'ld bring but little ayde, Sir.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: (I'le looke to you 'ifaith, Gamſter.) An vnfortunate fooliſh Tribe you are falne into, Lady, I wonder you can endure 'hem.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Sir, they that cannot worke their fetters off; muſt weare 'hem.

sp.speaker: WIN W.
sp.p: You ſee what care they haue on you, to leaue you thus.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Faith the ſame they haue of themſelues, Sir. I cannot greatly complaine, if this were all the plea I had againſt 'hem.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p:
'Tis true! but will you pleaſe to withdraw with vs, a little, and make them thinke, they haue loſt you. I hope our manners ha' beene ſuch hitherto, and our language, as will giue
you no cauſe, to doubt your ſelfe, in our company.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Sir, I will giue my ſelfe, no cauſe; I am ſo ſecure of mine owne manners, as I ſuſpect not yours.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Looke where Iohn Little-wit comes.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Away, I'le not be ſeene, by him.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: No, you were not beſt, hee'ld tell his mother, the widdow.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Heatt, what doe you meane?

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Cry you mercy, is the winde there? muſt not the widdow be nam'd?

div.head: ACT. III
SCENE. VI.
IOHN. WIN. TRASH. LEATHERHEAD. KNOCKHVM. BVSY. PVRECRAFT.

sp.p: DOe you heare Win, Win?

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: What ſay you, Iohn?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: While they are paying the reckoning, Win, I'll tell you a thing Win, wee ſhall neuer ſee any ſights i'the Fayre, Win, except you long ſtill, Win, good Win, ſweet Win, long to ſee ſome Hobby-horſes, and ſome Drummes, and Rattles, and Dogs, and fine deuices, Win. The Bull with the fiue legs, Win; and the great Hog: now you ha' begun with Pigge, you may long for any thing, Win, and ſo for my Motion, Win.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: But we ſha'not eat o'the Bull, and the Hogge, Iohn, how ſhall I long then?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: O yes! Win: you may long to ſee, as well as to taſte, Win: how did the Pothecarie's wife, Win, that long'd to ſee the Anatomy, Win? or the Lady, Win, that deſir'd to ſpit i'the great Lawyers mouth, after an eloquent pleading? I aſſure you they long'd, VVin, good Win, goe in, and long.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: I think we are rid of our new cuſtomer, brother Leatherhead, wee ſhall heare no more of him. They plot to be gone.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: All the better, let's packe vp all, and be gone, before he finde vs.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: Stay a little, yonder comes a company: it may be wee may take ſome more money.

sp.speaker: KNO,
sp.p: Sir, I will take your counſell, and cut my haire, and leaue vapours: I ſee, that Tabacco, and Bottle-Ale, and Pig, and Whit, and very Vrſla, her ſelfe, is all vanity.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p:
Onely Pigge was not comprehended in my admonition,
the reſt were. For long haire, it is an Enſigne of pride, a banner, and the world is full of thoſe banners, very full of Banners. And, bottle-ale is a drinke of Sathan's, a diet-drinke of Sathans, deuiſed to puffe vs vp, and make vs ſwell in this latter age of vanity, as the ſmoake of tabacco, to keepe vs in miſt and error: But the fleſhly woman, (which you call Vrſla) is aboue all to be auoyded, hauing the marks vpon her, of the three enemies of Man, the World, as being in the Faire; the Deuill, as being in the fire; and and the Fleſh, as being her ſelfe.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Brother Zeale-of-the-land! what ſhall we doe? my daughter Win-the-fight, is falne into her fit of longing againe.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: For more pig? there is no more, is there?

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: To ſee ſome ſights, i' the Faire.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Siſter, let her fly the impurity of the place, ſwiftly, leſt ſhee partake of the pitch thereof. Thou art the ſeate of the Beaſt, O Smithfield, and I will leaue thee. Idolatry peepeth out on euery ſide of thee.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: An excellent right Hypocrite! now his belly is full, he falls a railing and kicking, the Iade. A very good vapour! I'll in, and ioy Vrſla, with telling, how her pigge works, two and a halfe he eate to his ſhare. And he has drunke a pailefull. He eates with his eyes, as well as his teeth.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: What doe you lack, Gentlemen? What is't you buy? Rattles, Drumms, Babies.—

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Peace, with thy Apocryphall wares, thou prophane Publican: thy Bells, thy Dragons, and thy Tobie's Dogges. Thy Hobby-horſe is an Idoll, a very Idoll, a feirce and rancke Idoll: And thou, the Nabuchadnezzar, the proud Nabuchadnezzar of the Faire, that ſet'ſt it vp, for children to fall downe to, and worſhip.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Cry you mercy, Sir, will you buy a fiddle to fill vp your noiſe.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Looke Win. doe, looke a Gods name, and ſaue your longing. Here be fine ſights.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: I child, ſo you hate 'hem, as our Brother Zeale do's, you may looke on 'hem.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Or what do you ſay, to a Drumme. Sir?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: It is the broken belly of the Beaſt, and thy Bellowes there are his lungs, and theſe Pipes are his throate, thoſe Feathers are of his taile, and thy Rattles, the gnaſhing of his teeth.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: And what's my ginger-bread? I pray you.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: The prouander that pricks him vp. Hence with thy baſket of Popery, thy neſt of Images: and whole legend of ginger-worke.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Sir if you be not quiet, the quicklier, I'll ha' you clapp'd fairely by the heeles, for diſturbing the Faire.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: The ſinne of the Faire prouokes me, I cannot bee ſilent.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Good brother Zeale!

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Sir, I'll make you ſilent, beleeue it.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Il'd giue a ſhilling, you could i'faith, friend.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Sir, giue me your ſhilling, I'll giue you my ſhop, if I do not, and I'll leaue it in pawne with you, i'the meane time.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: A match i'faith, but do it quickly, then.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Hinder me not, woman. I was mou'd in ſpirit, He speakes to the widdow. to bee here, this day, in this Faire, this wicked, and foule Faire; and fitter may it be a called a foule, then a Faire: To proteſt againſt the abuſes of it, the foule abuſes of it, in regard of the afflicted Saints, that are troubled, very much troubled, exceedingly troubled, with the opening of the merchandize of Babylon againe, & the peeping of Popery vpon the ſtals, here, here, in the high places. See you not Goldylocks, the purple ſtrumpet, there? in her yellow gowne, and greene ſleeues? the prophane pipes, the tinckling timbrells? A ſhop of reliques!

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Pray you forbeare, I am put in truſt with 'hem.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: And this Idolatrous Groue of Images, this flasket of Idols!Ouerthrows the ginger-bread. which I will pull downe—

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: O my ware, my ware, God bleſſe it.)

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: In my zeale, and glory to be thus exercis'd.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Here he is, pray you lay hold on his zeale, wee cannot ſell a whiſtle, for him, in tune. Stop his noyſe, firſt!

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Thou canſt not: 'tis a ſanctified noiſe. Leatherhead enters with officers I will make a loud and moſt ſtrong noiſe, till I haue daunted the prophane enemy. And for this cauſe.—

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Sir, heer's no man afraid of you, or your cauſe. You ſhall ſweare it, i'the ſtocks, Sir.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: I will thruſt my ſelfe into the ſtocks, vpon the pikes of the Land.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Carry him away.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: What doe you meane, wicked men?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Let them alone; I feare them not.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Was not this ſhilling well ventur'd, Win? for our liberty? Now we may goe play, and ſee ouer the Fayre, where we liſt our ſelues; my mother is gone after him, and let her ee'n go, and looſe vs.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Yes Iohn, but I know not what to doe.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: For what, Win?

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: For a thing, I am aſham'd to tell you, i'faith, and 'tis too farre to go home.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: I pray thee bee not aſham'd, VVin. Come, i'faith thou ſhall not be aſham'd, is it any thing about the Hobby-horſe-man? an't be, ſpeake freely.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Hang him, baſe Bobehin, I ſcorne him; no, I haue very great, what ſha'call'um, Iohn.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p:
ô! Is that all, Win? wee'll goe backe to Captaine Iordan; to the pig-womans, Win. hee'll helpe vs, or ſhe with a
〈1 page duplicate〉
〈1 page duplicate〉
dripping pan, or an old kettle, or ſomething. The poore greaſie ſoule loues you, Win, and after we'll viſit the Fayre all ouer, Win, and, ſee my Puppet play, Win, you know it's a fine matter, Win.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Let's away, I counſell'd you to packe vp afore, Ione.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.p: A poxe of his Bedlem purity. Hee has ſpoyl'd halfe my ware: but the beſt is, wee loſe nothing, if wee miſſe our firſt Merchant.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: It ſhall be hard for him to finde, or know vs, when we are tranſlated, Ione.

div.head: ACT. IIII.
SCENE. I.
TROVBLE-ALL. BRISTLE. HAGGISE, COKES. IVSTICE. POCHER. BVSY. PVRECRAFT.

sp.p: MY Maſters, I doe make no doubt, but you are officers.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: What then, Sir?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: And the Kings louing, and obedient ſubiects.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Obedient, friend? take heede what you ſpeake, I aduiſe you: Oliuer Briſtle aduiſes you. His louing ſubiects, we grant you: but not his obedient, at this time, by your leaue, wee know our ſelues, a little better then ſo, wee are to command, Sr. and ſuch as you are to be obedient. Here's one of his obedient ſubiects, going to the ſtocks, and wee'll make you ſuch another, if you talke.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: You are all wiſe enough i'your places, I know.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: If you know it, Sir, why doe you bring it in queſtion?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: I queſtion nothing, pardon me. I do only hope you haue warrant, He goes away againe. for what you doe, and ſo, quit you, and ſo, multiply you.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: What's hee? bring him vp to the ſtocks there. Why bring you him not vp?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: If you haue Iuſtice Ouerdoo's warrant, 'tis well:comes again. you are ſafe; that is the warrant of warrants. I'le not giue this button, for any mans warrant elſe.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Like enough, Sir, but let me tell you, an' you play away your buttons, thus, you will want 'hem ere night, goes away. for any ſtore I ſee about you: you might keepe 'hem, and ſaue pinnes, I wuſſe.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: What ſhould hee be, that doth ſo eſteeme, and aduance my warrant? he ſeemes a ſober and diſcreet perſon! it is a comfort to a good conſcience, to be follow'd with a good fame, in his ſufferings. The world will haue a pretty taſt by this, how I can beare aduerſity: and it will beget a kind of reuerence, toward me, hereafter, euen from mine enemies, when they ſhall ſee I carry my calamity nobly, and that it doth neither breake mee, nor bend mee.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: Come, Sir, heere's a place for you to preach in. They put him in the ſtocks. Will you put in your legge?

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: That I will, cheerefully.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: O' my conſcience a Seminary!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well my Maſters, I'le leaue him with you; now I ſee him beſtow'd, I'le goe looke for my goods, and Numps.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: You may, Sir, I warrant you; where's the tother Bawler? fetch him too, you ſhall find 'hem both faſt enough.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: In the mid'ſt of this tumult, I will yet be the Author of mine owne reſt, and not minding their fury, ſit in the ſtockes, in that calme, as ſhall be able to trouble a Triumph. comes again,

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Doe you aſſure me vpon your words? may I vndertake for you, if I be ask'd the queſtion; that you haue this warrant?

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: What's this fellow, for Gods ſake?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Doe but ſhew me Adam Ouerdoo, and I am ſatisfied. goes out.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Hee is a fellow that is diſtracted, they ſay; one Trouble-all: hee was an officer in the Court of Pie-poulders, here laſt yeere, and put out on his place by Iuſtice Ouerdoo.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Ha!

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Vpon which, he tooke an idle conceipt, and's runne mad vpon't. So that euer ſince, hee will doe nothing, but by Iuſtice Ouerdoo's warrant, he will not eate a cruſt, nor drinke a little, nor make him in his apparell, ready. His wife, Sirreuerence, cannot get him make his water, or ſhift his ſhirt, without his warrant.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: If this be true, this is my greateſt diſaſter! how am I bound to ſatisfie this poore man, that is of ſo good a nature to mee, out of his wits! where there is no roome left for diſſembling. comes in.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: If you cannot ſhew me Adam Ouerdoo, I am in doubt of you: I am afraid you cannot anſwere it. goes againe.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: Before me, Neighbour Briſtle (and now I thinke on't better) Iuſtice Ouerdoo, is a very parantory perſon.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: O! are you aduis'd of that? and a ſeuere Iuſticer, by your leaue.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Doe I heare ill o'that ſide, too?

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: He will ſit as vpright o'the bench, an' you marke him, as a candle i'the ſocket, and giue light to the whole Court in euery buſineſſe.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: But he will burne blew, and ſwell like a bile (God bleſſe vs) an' he be angry.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: I, and hee will be angry too, when his liſt, that's more: and when hee is angry, be it right or wrong; hee has the Law on's ſide, euer. I marke that too.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I will be more tender hereafter. I ſee compaſſion may become a Iuſtice, though it be a weakneſſe, I confeſſe; and neerer a vice, then a vertue.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: They take the Iuſtice out. Well, take him out o' the ſtocks againe, wee'll goe a ſure way to worke, wee'll ha' the Ace of hearts of our ſide, if we can.

sp.speaker: POC.
sp.p: Come, bring him away to his fellow, there. Maſter Buſy, we ſhall rule your legges, I hope, though wee cannot rule your tongue.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: No, Miniſter of darkeneſſe, no, thou canſt not rule my tongue, my tongue it is mine own, and with it I will both knocke, and mocke downe your Bartholmew-abhominations, till you be made a hiſſing to the neighbour Pariſhes, round about.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: Let him alone, we haue deuis'd better vpon't.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: And ſhall he not into the ſtocks then?

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: No, Miſtreſſe, wee'll haue 'hem both to Iuſtice Ouerdoo, and let him doe ouer 'hem as is fitting. Then I, and my goſſip Haggis, and my beadle Pocher are diſcharg'd.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: O, I thanke you, bleſſed, honeſt men!

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Nay, neuer thank vs, but thank this mad-man that comes heere, hee put it in our heads.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Is hee mad? Now heauen increaſe his madneſſe, and bleſſe it, Comes againe. and thanke it, Sir, your poore hand-maide thanks you.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Haue you a warrant? an' you haue a warrant, ſhew it.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Yes, I haue a warrant out of the word, to giue thankes for remouing any ſcorne intended to the brethren.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: It is Iuſtice Ouerdoo's warrant, that I looke for, if you haue not that, keepe your word, I'le keepe mine. Quit yee, and multiply yee.
COme away Nightingale, I pray thee.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Whither goe you? where's your warrant?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Warrant, for what, Sir?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: For what you goe about, you know how fit it is, an' you haue no warrant, bleſſe you, I'le pray for you, Goes out. that's all I can doe.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: What meanes hee?

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: A mad-man that haunts the Fayre, doe you not know him? it's maruell hee has not more followers, after his ragged heeles.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Beſhrew him, he ſtartled me: I thought he had knowne of our plot. Guilt's a terrible thing! ha' you prepar'd the Coſtardmonger?

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: Yes, and agreed for his basket of peares; hee is at the corner here, ready. And your Priſe, he comes downe, ſailing, that way, all alone; without his Protector: hee is rid of him, it ſeemes.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: I, I know; I ſhould ha' follow'd his Protector-ſhip for a feat I am to doe vpon him: But this offer'd it ſelfe, ſo i'the way, I could not let it ſcape: heere he comes, whiſtle, Nightingale whiſtles be this ſport call'd Dorring the Dottrell.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: Wh, wh, wh, wh, &c.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: By this light, I cannot finde my ginger-bread-Wife, nor my Hobby-horſe-man in all the Fayre, now; to ha' my money againe. And I do not know the way out on't, to go home for more, doe you heare, friend, you that whiſtle; what tune is that, you whiſtle?

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: A new tune, I am practiſing, Sir.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Doſt thou know where I dwell, I pray thee? nay, on with thy tune, I ha' no ſuch haſt, for an anſwer: I'le practiſe with thee. Nightingale ſets his foots afore him, and he falls with his basket.

sp.speaker: COS.
sp.p: Buy any peares, very fine peares, peares fine.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Gods ſo! a muſſe, a muſſe, a muſſe, a muſſe.

sp.speaker: COS.
sp.p: Good Gentleman, my ware, my ware, I am a poore man. Good Sir, my ware.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: Cokes falls aſcrambling whileſt they runne away with his things. Let me hold your ſword, Sir, it troubles you.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Doe, and my cloake, an'thou wilt; and my hat, too.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: A delicate great boy! me thinks, he out-ſcrambles 'hem all. I cannot perſwade my ſelfe, but he goes to grammer-ſchole yet; and playes the trewant, to day.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: Would he had another purſe to cut, Zekiel.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Purſe? a man might cut out his kidneys, I thinke; and he neuer feele 'hem, he is ſo earneſt at the ſport.

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: His ſoule is halfe way out on's body, at the game.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Away, Nightingale: that way.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I thinke I am furniſh'd for Catherne peares, for one vnder-meale: gi'me my cloake.

sp.speaker: COS.
sp.p: Good Gentleman, giue me my ware.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Where's the fellow, I ga' my cloake to? my cloake? and my hat?He runs out. ha! Gods'lid, is he gone? thieues, thieues, helpe me to cry, Gentlemen.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Away, Coſtermonger, come to vs to Vrſla's. Talke of him to haue a ſoule? 'heart, if hee haue any more then a thing giuen him in ſtead of ſalt, onely to keepe him from ſtinking, I'le be hang'd afore my time, preſently, where ſhould it be trow? in his blood? hee has not ſo much to'ard it in his whole body, as will maintaine a good Flea; And if hee take this courſe, he will not ha' ſo much land left, as to reare a Calfe within this twelue mouth. Was there euer greene Plouer ſo pull'd! That his little Ouerſeer had beene heere now, and beene but tall enough, to ſee him ſteale peares, in exchange, for his beauer-hat, and his cloake thus? I muſt goe finde him out, next, for his blacke boxe, and his Patent (it feemes) hee has of his place; which I thinke the Gentleman would haue a reuerſion of; that ſpoke to me for it ſo earneſtly.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: He comes againe. Would I might loſe my doublet, and hoſe, too; as I am an honeſt man, and neuer ſtirre, if I thinke there be any thing, but thieuing, and cooz'ning, i'this whole Fayre, Bartholmew-fayre, quoth he; an' euer any Bartholmew had that lucke in't, that I haue had, I'le be martyr'd for him, throws away his peares. and in Smithfield, too. I ha' paid for my peares, a rot on 'hem, I'le keepe 'hem no longer; you were choake-peares to mee; I had bin better ha' gone to mum chance for you, I wuſſe. Me thinks the Fayre ſhould not haue vs'd me thus, and 'twere but for my names ſake, I would nor ha' vs'd a dog o'the name, ſo. O, Numps will triumph, now! Friend, doe you know who I am? or where I lye? I doe not my ſelfe, I'll beſworne. Doe but carry me home, and I'le pleaſe thee, I ha' money enough there, I ha' loſt my ſelfe, and my cloake and my hat; and my fine ſword, and my ſiſter, and Numps, and Miſtris Grace, (a Gentlewoman that I ſhould ha' marryed) and a cut-worke handkercher, ſhee ga' mee, and two purſes to day. And my bargaine. o'Hobby-horſes and Ginger-bread, Trouble-all comes again. which grieues me worſt of all.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: By whoſe warrant, Sir, haue you done all this?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Warrant? thou art a wiſe fellow, indeed, as if a man need a warrant to loſe any thing, with.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Yes, Iuſtice Ouerdo's warrant, a man may get, and loſe with, I'le ſtand to't.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Iuſtice Ouerdoo? Doſt thou know him? I lye there, hee is my brother in Law, hee marryed my ſiſter: pray thee ſhew me the way, doſt thou know the houſe?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Sir, ſhew mee your warrant, I know nothing without a warrant, pardon me.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Why, I warrant thee, come along: thou ſhalt ſee, I haue wrought pillowes there, and cambricke ſheetes, and ſweete bags, too. Pray thee guide me to the houſe.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Sir, I'le tell you; goe you thither your ſelfe, firſt, alone; tell your worſhipfull brother your minde; and but bring me three lines of his hand, or his Clerkes, with Adam Ouerdoo, vnderneath; here I'le ſtay you, Ile obey you, and I'le guide you preſently.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: S'lid, this is an Aſſe, I ha' found him, poxe vpon mee, what doe I talking to ſuch a dull foole; farewell, you are a very Coxcomb, doe you heare?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: I thinke, I am, if Iuſtice Ouerdoo ſigne to it, I am, and ſo wee are all, hee'll quit vs all, multiply vs all.

div.head: ACT. IIII.
SCENE. IIJ.
GRACE. QVARLOVS. VVIN-WIFE. They enter with their ſwords drawne. TROVBLE-ALL. EDGVVORTH.

sp.p: GEntlemen, this is no way that you take: you do but breed one another trouble, and offence, and giue me no contentment at all. I am no ſhe, that affects to be quarell'd for, or haue my name or fortune made the queſtion of mens ſwords.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: S'lood, wee loue you.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: If you both loue mee, as you pretend, your owne reaſon will tell you, but one can enioy me; and to that point, there leads a directer line, then by my infamy, which muſt follow, if you fight. 'Tis true, I haue profeſt it to you ingenuouſly, that rather then to be yoak'd with this Bridegroome is appointed me, I would take vp any husband, almoſt vpon any truſt. Though Subtilty would ſay to me, (I know) hee is a foole, and has an eſtate, and I might gouerne him, and enioy a friend, beſide. But theſe are not my aymes, I muſt haue a husband I muſt loue, or I cannot liue with him. I ſhall ill make one of theſe politique wiues!

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Why, if you can like either of vs, Lady, ſay, which is he, and the other ſhall ſweare inſtantly to deſiſt.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Content, I accord to that willingly.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Sure you thinke me a woman of an extreme leuity, Gentlemen, or a ſtrange fancy, that (meeting you by chance in ſuch a place, as this, both at one inſtant, and not yet of two hours acquaintance, neither of you deſeruing afore the other, of me) I ſhould ſo forſake my modeſty (though I might affect one more particularly) as to ſay, This is be, and name him.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Why, wherefore ſhould you not? What ſhould hinder you?

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: If you would not giue it to my modeſty, allow it yet to my wit; giue me ſo much of woman, and cunning, as not to betray my ſelfe impertinently. How can I iudge of you, ſo farre as to a choyſe, without knowing you more? you are both equall, and alike to mee, yet: and ſo indifferently affected by mee, as each of you might be the man, if the other were away. For you are reaſonable creatures, you haue vnderſtanding, and diſcourſe. And if fate ſend me an vnderſtanding husband, I haue no feare at all, but mine owne manners ſhall make him a good one.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Would I were put forth to making for you, then.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: It may be you are, you know not what's toward you: will you conſent to a motion of mine, Gentlemen?

sp.speaker: WIN W.
sp.p: What euer it be, we'll preſume reaſonableneſſe, comming from you.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: And fitneſſe, too.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: I ſaw one of you buy a paire of tables, e'en now.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Yes, heere they be, and maiden ones too, vnwritten in.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: The fitter for what they may be imployed in. You ſhall write either of you, heere, a word, or a name, what you like beſt; but of two, or three ſyllables at moſt: and the next perſon that comes this way (becauſe Deſtiny has a high hand in buſineſſe of this nature) I'le demand, which of the two words, he, or ſhe doth approue; and according to that ſentence, fixe my reſolution, and affection, without change.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Agreed, my word is conceiued already.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: And mine ſhall not be long creating after.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: But you ſhall promiſe, Gentlemen, not to be curious to know, which of you it is, taken; but giue me leaue to conceale that till you haue brought me, either home, or where I may ſafely tender my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: WIN-W
sp.p: Why that's but equall.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Wee are pleas'd.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Becauſe I will bind both your indeauours to work together, friendly, and ioyntly, each to the others fortune, and haue my ſelfe ſitted with ſome meanes, to make him that is forſaken, a part of amends.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Theſe conditions are very curteous. Well my word is out of the Arcadia, then: Argalus.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: And mine out of the play, Palemon.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Haue you any warrant for this, Gentlemen?Trouble-all comes again.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: WIN-W. Ha!

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: There muſt be a warrant had, beleeue it.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: For what?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Fot whatſoeuer it is, any thing indeede, no matter what.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: S'light, here's a fine ragged Prophet, dropt downe 'ithe nicke!

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Heauen quit you, Gentlemen.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Nay, ſtay a little, good Lady, put him to the queſtion.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: You are content, then?

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: QVAR. Yes yes.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Sir, heere are two names written—

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Is Iudice Ouerdoo, one?

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: How, Sir? I pray you read 'hem to your ſelfe, it is for a wager betweene theſe Gentlemen, and with a ſtroake or any difference, marke which you approue beſt.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: They may be both worſhipfull names for ought I know, Miſtreſſe, but Adam Ouerdoo had beene worth three of 'hem, I aſſure you, in this place, that's in plaine engliſh.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: This man amazes mee! I pray you, like one of 'hem, Sir.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: I doe like him there, that has the beſt warrant, Miſtreſſe, to ſaue your longing, and (multiply him) It may be this. But I am I ſtill for Iuſtice Ouerdoo, that's my conſcience. And quit you.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Is't done, Lady?

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: I, and ſtrangely, as euer I ſaw! What fellow is this trow?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: No matter what, a Fortune-teller wee ha' made him. Which is't, which is't.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Nay, did you not promiſe, not to enquire?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: S'lid, I forgot that, pray you pardon mee. Looke, here's our Mercury come: The Licence arriues i'the fineſt time, too! 'tis but ſcraping out Cokes his name, and 'tis done.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: How now lime-twig? haſt thou touch'd.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p:
Not yet, Sir, except you would goe with mee, and ſee't, it's not worth ſpeaking on. The act is nothing, without a witneſſe. Yonder he is, your man with the boxe falne into the fineſt company, and ſo tranſported with vapours, they ha' got in a Northren Clothier, and one Puppy, a Weſterne man, that's come to wraſtle before my Lord Maior, anone, and Captaine Whit, and one Val Cutting, that helpes Captaine Iordan to roare, a circling boy: with whom your Numps, is ſo taken, that you may ſtrip him of his cloathes, if you will. I'le vndertake to geld him for you; if you had but a Surgeon, ready, to ſeare him. And Miſtreſſe Iuſtice,
there, is the goodeſt woman! ſhee do's ſo loue 'hem all ouer, in termes of Iuſtice, and the Stile of authority, with her hood vpright — that I beſeech you come away Gentlemen, and ſee't.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: S light, I would not loſe it for the Fayre, what'll you doe, Ned?

sp.speaker: WIN W.
sp.p: Why, ſtay heere about for you, Miſtreſſe Welborne muſt not be ſeene.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Doe ſo, and find out a Prieſt i'the meane time, I'le bring the Licenſe. Lead, which way is't?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Here, Sir, you are o'the backeſide o'the Booth already, you may heare the noiſe.

div.head: ACT. IIIJ.
SCENE. IV.
KNOCKHVM. NORDERN. PVPPY. CVTTING. WHIT. EDGVVORTH. QVARLOVS. OVER DOO. WASPE. BRISTLE.

sp.p: VVHit, bid Vall Cutting continue the vapours for a lift, Whit, for a lift.

sp.speaker: NOR.
sp.p: Il'e ne mare, Il'e ne mare, the eale's too meeghty.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: How now! my Galloway Nag, the ſtaggers? ha! Whit, gi'him a ſlit i'the fore-head. Cheare vp, man, a needle, and threed to ſtitch his eares. I'ld cure him now an' I had it, with a little butter, and garlike, long-pepper, and graines. Where's my horne? I'le gi'him a maſh, preſently, ſhall take away this dizzineſſe.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: Why, where are you zurs? doe you vlinch, and leaue vs i'the zuds, now?

sp.speaker: NOR.
sp.p: I'le ne mare, I'is e'en as vull as a Paipers bag, by my troth, I.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: Doe my Northerne cloth zhrinke i'the wetting? ha?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Why, well ſaid, old Flea-bitten, thou'lt neuer tyre, I ſee.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: They fall to their vapours, againe. No, Sir, but he may tire, if it pleaſe him.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Who told dee ſho? that he vuld neuer teer, man?

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: No matter who told him ſo, ſo long as he knowes.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Nay, I know nothing, Sir, pardon me there.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: They are at it ſtil, Sir, this they call vapours.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: He ſhall not pardon dee, Captaine, dou ſhalt not be pardon'd. Pre'de ſhweete heart doe not pardon him.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: S'light, I'le pardon him, an'I liſt, whoſoeuer ſaies nay to't.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Where's Numps? I miſſe him. Here they continue their game of vapours, which is non ſenſe. Euery man to oppoſe the laſt man that ſpoke: whethe it concern'd him, or no.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why, I ſay nay to't.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: O there he is!

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: To what doe you ſay nay, Sir?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: To any thing, whatſoeuer it is, ſo long as I do not like it.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Pardon me, little man, dou muſht like it a little.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: No, hee muſt not like it at all, Sir, there you are i'the wrong.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: I tinke I be, he muſht not like it, indeede.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Nay, then he both muſt, and will like it, Sir, for all you.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: If he haue reaſon, he may like it, Sir.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: By no meanſh Captaine, vpon reaſon, he may like nothing vpon reaſon.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I haue no reaſon, nor I will heare of no reaſon, nor I will looke for no reaſon, and he is an Aſſe, that either knowes any, or lookes for't from me.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Yes, in ſome ſenſe you may haue reaſon, Sir.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I, in ſome ſenſe, I care not if I grant you.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Pardon mee, thou ougſht to grant him nothing, in no ſhenſh, if dou doe loue dy ſhelfe, angry man.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why then, I doe grant him nothing; and I haue no ſenſe.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: 'Tis true, thou haſt no ſenſe indeed.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: S'lid, but I haue ſenſe, now I thinke on't better, and I will grant him any thing, doe you ſee?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: He is i'the right, and do's vtter a ſufficient vapour.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Nay, it is no ſufficient vapour, neither, I deny that.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Then it is a ſweet vapour.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: It may be a ſweet vapour.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nay, it is no ſweet vapour, neither, Sir, it ſtinkes, and I'le ſtand to't.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yes, I tinke it doſh ſhtinke, Captaine. All vapour doſh ſhtinke.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nay, then it do's not ſtinke, Sir, and it ſhall not ſtinke.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: By your leaue, it may, Sir.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I, by my leaue, it may ſtinke, I know that.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Pardon me, thou knoweſht nothing, it cannot by thy leaue, angry man.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: How can it not?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Nay, neuer queſtion him, for he is i'the right.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yeſh, I am i'de right, I confeſh it, ſo iſh de little man too.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I'le haue nothing confeſt, that concernes mee. I am not i'the right, nor neuer was i'the right, nor neuer will be i'the right, while I am in my right minde,

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Minde? why, heere's no man mindes you, Sir, They drinke againe. nor any thing elſe.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: Vreind, will you mind this that wee doe?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Call you this vapours? this is ſuch beltching of quarrell, as I neuer heard. Will you minde your buſineſſe, Sir?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: You ſhall ſee, Sir.

sp.speaker: NOR.
sp.p: I'le ne maire, my waimb warkes too mickle with this auready.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Will you take that, Maſter Waſpe, that no body ſhould minde you?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why? what ha' you to doe? is't any matter to you?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: No, but me thinks you ſhould not be vnminded, though,

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nor, I wu'not be, now I thinke on't, doe you heare, new acquaintance, do's no man mind me, ſay you?

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Yes, Sir, euery man heere mindes you, but how?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nay, I care as little how, as you doe, that was not my queſtion.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: No, noting was ty queſtion, tou art a learned man, and I am a valiant man, i'faith la, tou ſhalt ſpeake for mee, and I vill fight for tee.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Fight for him, Whit? A groſſe vapour, hee can fight for himſelfe.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: It may be I can, but it may be, I wu' not, how then?

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Why, then you may chuſe.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why, and I'le chuſe whether I'le chuſe or no.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: I thinke you may, and 'tis true; and I allow it for a reſolute vapour.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nay, then, I doe thinke you doe not thinke, and it is no reſolute vapour.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Yes, in ſome ſort he may allow you.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: In no ſort, Sir, pardon me, I can allow him nothing. You miſtake the vapour.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: He miſtakes nothing, Sir, in no ſort.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yes, I pre dee now, let him miſtake.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: A turd i'your teeth, neuer pre dee mee, for I will haue nothing miſtaken.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: They fall by the eares. Turd, ha turd? a noyſome vapour, ſtrike Whit.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: Why, Gentlemen, why Gentlemen, I charge you vpon my authority, conſerue the peace. In the Kings name, and my Husbands, put vp your weapons, I ſhall be driuen to commit you my ſelfe, elſe:

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Ha, ha, ha.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Why doe you laugh, Sir?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Sir, you'll allow mee my chriſtian liberty. I may laugh, I hope.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: In ſome ſort you may, and in ſome ſort you may not, Sir.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Nay in ſome ſort, Sir, hee may neither laugh, nor hope, in this company.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Yes, then he may both laugh, and hope in any ſort, an't pleaſe him.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Faith, and I will then, for it doth pleaſe mee exceedingly.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: No exceeding neither, Sir.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: No, that vapour is too lofty.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Gentlemen, I doe not play well at your game of vapours, I am not very good at it, but—

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Doe you heare, Sir? I would ſpeake with you in circle?Hee drawes a circle on the ground.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: In circle, Sir? what would you with me in circle?

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Can you lend me a Piece, a Iacobus? in circle?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: S'lid, your circle will proue more coſtly then your vapours, then. Sir, no, I lend you none.

sp.speaker: CVT.
sp.p: Your beard's not well turn'd vp, Sir.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: How Raſcall? are you playing with my beard?They draw all, and fight. I'le breake circle with you.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: NOR. Gentlemen, Gentlemen!

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Gather vp, Whit, gather vp, Whit, good vapours.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: What meane you? are you Rebells? Gentlemen? ſhall I ſend out a Serieant at Armes, or a Writ o'Rebellion, againſt you? I'le commit you vpon my woman-hood, for a Riot, vpon my Iuſtice-hood, if you perſiſt.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Vpon your Iuſtice-hood? Mary ſhite o'your hood, you'll commit? Spoke like a true Iuſtice of peace's wife, indeed, and a fine female Lawyer! turd i'your teeth for a fee, now.

sp.speaker: OVER.
sp.p: Why, Numps, in Maſter Ouerdoo's name, I charge you.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Good Miſtreſſe Vnderdoo hold your tongue.

sp.speaker: OVER.
sp.p: Alas! poore Numps.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Alas! and why alas from you, I beſeech you? or why poore Numps, goody Rich? am I come to be pittied by your tuft taffata now? why Miſtreſſe, I knew Adam, the Clerke, your husband, when he was Adam Scriuener, and writ for two pence a ſheet, as high as he beares his head now, or you your hood, Dame. What are you, Sir?The watch comes in.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Wee be men, and no Infidells; what is the matter, here, and the noyſes? can you tell?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Heart, what ha' you to doe? cannot a man quarrell in quietneſſe? but hee muſt be put out on't by you? what are you?

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Why, wee be his Maieſties Watch, Sir.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Watch? S'blood, you are a ſweet watch, indeede. A body would thinke, and you watch'd well a nights, you ſhould be contented to ſleepe at this time a day. Get you to your fleas, and your flocke-beds, you Rogues, your kennells, and lye downe cloſe.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Downe? yes, we will downe, I warrant you, downe with him in his Maieſties name, downe, downe with him, and carry him away, to the pigeon-holes.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: I thanke you honeſt friends, in the behalfe o'the Crowne, and the peace, and in Maſter Ouerdoo's name, for ſuppreſſing enormities.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Stay, Briſtle, heere iſh a noder braſh o'drunkards, but very quiet, ſpeciall drunkards, will pay dee, fiue ſhillings very well. Take 'hem to dee, in de graiſh o' God▪ one of hem do's change cloth, for Ale in the Fayre, here, te toder iſh a ſtrong man, a mighty man, my Lord Mayors man, and a wraſtler. Hee has wraſhled ſo long with the bottle, heere, that the man with the beard, haſh almoſht ſtreeke vp hiſh heelſh.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: S'lid, the Clerke o'the Market, has beene to cry him all the Fayre ouer, here, for my Lords ſeruice.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Tere he iſh, pre de taik him henſh, and make ty beſt on him. How now woman o' ſhilke, vat ailſh ty ſhweet faiſh? art tou melancholy?

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: A little diſtemper'd with theſe enormities; ſhall I intreat a curteſie of you, Captaine?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Intreat a hundred, veluet voman, I vill doe it, ſhpeake out.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: I cannot with modeſty ſpeake it out, but—

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: I vill doe it, and more, and more, for dee. What Vrſla, and't be bitch, and't be baud and't be!

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: How now Raſcall? what roare you for? old Pimpe.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Heere, put vp de cloakes Vrſh; de purchaſe, pre dee now, ſhweet Vrſh, help dis good braue voman, to a Iordan, and't be.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: S'lid call your Captaine Iordan to her, can you not?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Nay, pre dee leaue dy conſheits, and bring the veluet woman to de—

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: I bring her, hang her: heart muſt I find a common pot for euery punque i'your purlews?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: O good voordſh, Vrſh, it iſh a gueſt o'veluet, i'fait la.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Let her ſell her hood, and buy a ſpunge, with a poxe to her, my veſſell, employed Sir. I haue but one, and 'tis the bottome of an old bottle. An honeſt Proctor, and his wife, are at it, within, if ſhee'll ſtay her time, ſo.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: As ſoone aſh tou canſht ſhwet Vrſh. Of a valiant man I tinke I am the patientſh man i'the world, or in all Smithfield.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: How now Whit? cloſe vapours, ſtealing your leaps? couering in corners, ha?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: No fait, Captaine, dough tou beeſht a viſhe man, dy vit is a mile hence, now. I vas procuring a ſhmall courteſie, for a woman of faſhion here.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: Yes, Captaine, though I am Iuſtice of peace's wife, I doe loue Men of warre, and the Sonnes of the ſword, when they come before my husband.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Say'ſt thou ſo Filly? thou ſhalt haue a leape preſently, I'le horſe thee my ſelfe, elſe.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Come, will you bring her in now? and let her talke her turne?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Gramercy good Vrſh, I tanke dee.

sp.speaker: OVER.
sp.p: Maſter Ouerdoo ſhall thanke her.

div.head: ACT. IIII.
SCENE. V.
IOHN. WIN. VRSLA. KNOCKHVM. WHIT. OVERDOO. ALES.

sp.p: Good Ga'mere Vrs; Win, and I, are exceedingly beholden to you, and to Captaine Iordan, and Captaine Whit. Win, I'le be bold to leaue you, i'this good company, Win: for halfe an houre, or ſo Win, while I goe, and ſee how my matter goes forward, and if the Puppets be perfect: and then I'le come & fetch you, Win.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Will you leaue me alone with two men, Iohn?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: I, they are honeſt Gentlmen Win, Captaine Iordan, and Captaine Whit, they'll vſe you very ciuilly, Win, God b'w'you, Win.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: What's her husband gone?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: On his falſe, gallop, Vrs, away.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: An' you be right Bartholmew-birds, now ſhew your ſelues ſo: we are vndone for want of fowle i'the Fayre, here. Here will be Zekiell Edgworth, and three or foure gallants, with him at night, and I ha' neither Plouer nor Qúaile for 'hem: perſwade this betweene you two, to become a Bird o'the game, while I worke the veluet woman, within, (as you call her.)

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: I conceiue thee, Vrs! goe thy waies, doeſt thou heare, Whit? is't not pitty, my delicate darke cheſtnut here, with the fine leane head, large fore-head, round eyes, euen mouth, ſharpe eares, long necke, thinne cr • ſt, cloſe withers, plaine backe, deepe ſides, ſhort fillets, and full flankes: with a round belly, a plumpe buttocke, large thighes, knit knees, ſtreight legges, ſhort pa •• ernes, ſmooth hoofes, and ſhort heeles; ſhould lead a dull honeſt womans life, that might liue the life of a Lady?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yes, by my fait, and trot, it is, Captaine: de honeſht womans life is a ſcuruy dull life, indeed, la.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: How, Sir? is an honeſt womans life a ſcuruy life?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yes fait, ſhweet heart, beleeue him, de leefe of a Bond-woman! but if dou vilt harken to me, I vill make tee a free-woman, and a Lady: dou ſhalt liue like a Lady, as te Captaine ſaiſh.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p:
I, and be honeſt too ſometimes: haue her wiers, and
her tires, her greene gownes, and veluet petticoates.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: I, and ride to Ware and Rumford i'dy Coaſh, ſheede Players, be in loue vit 'hem; ſup vit gallantſh, be drunke, and coſt de noting.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Braue vapours!

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: And lye by twenty on'hem, if dou pleaſh ſhweet heart.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: What, and be honeſt ſtill, that were fine ſport.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Tiſh common, ſhweet heart, tou may'ſt doe it by my hand: it ſhall be iuſtified to ty husbands faiſh, now: tou ſhalt be as honeſht as the skinne betweene his hornſh, la!

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Yes, and weare a dreſſing, top, and top-gallant, to compare with ere a husband on 'hem all, for a fore-top: it is the vapour of ſpirit in the wife, to cuckold, now adaies; as it is the vapour of faſhion, in the husband, not to ſuſpect. Your prying cat-eyed-citizen, is an abominable vapour.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Lord, what a foole haue I beene!

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Mend then, and doe euery ting like a Lady, heereafter, neuer know ty husband, from another man.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Nor any one man from another, but i'the darke.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: I, and then it iſh no diſhgraſh to know any man.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Helpe, helpe here.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: How now? what vapour's there?

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: O, you are a ſweet Ranger! and looke well to your walks. Yonder is your Punque of Turnbull, Ramping Ales, has falne vpon the poore Gentlewoman within, and pull'd her hood ouer her eares, Alice eners, beating he Iuſtice's wife. and her hayre through it.

sp.speaker: OVE.
sp.p: Helpe, helpe, i'the Kings name.

sp.speaker: ALE.
sp.p: A miſchiefe on you, they are ſuch as you are, that vndoe vs, and take our trade from vs, with your tuft-taffata hanches.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: How now Alice!

sp.speaker: ALE.
sp.p: The poore common whores can ha' no traffique, for the priuy rich ones; your caps and hoods of veluet, call away our cuſtomers, and lick the fat from vs.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Peace you foule ramping Iade, you—

sp.speaker: ALE.
sp.p: Od's foote, you Bawd in greace, are you talking?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: VVhy, Alice, I ſay.

sp.speaker: ALE.
sp.p: Thou Sow of Smithfield, thou.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: Thou tripe of Turnebull.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Cat-a-mountaine-vapours! ha!

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: You know where you were taw'd lately, both laſh'd, and ſlaſh'd you were in Bridewell.

sp.speaker: ALE.
sp.p: I, by the ſame token, you rid that weeke, and broake out the bottome o'the Cart, Night-tub.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: VVhy, Lyon face! ha! doe you know who I am? ſhall I teare ruffe, ſlit waſtcoat, make ragges of petticoat? ha! goe to, vaniſh, for feare of vapours. Whit, a kick, Whit, in the parting vapour. Come braue woman, take a good heart, thou ſhalt be a Lady, too.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yes fait, dey ſhal all both be Ladies, and write Madame. I vill do't my ſelfe for dem. Doe, is the vord, and D is the middle letter of Madame, D D, put 'hem together, and make deeds, without which, all words are alike, la.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: 'Tis true, Vrſla, take 'hem in, open thy wardrope, and fit 'hem to their calling. Greene-gownes, Crimſon-petticoats, green women! my Lord Maiors green women! gueſts o'the Game, true bred. I'le prouide you a Coach, to take the ayre, in.

sp.speaker: VVIN.
sp.p: But doe you thinke you can get one?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: O, they are as common as wheelebarrowes, where there are great dunghills.
