Euery Pettifoggers wife, has 'hem, for firſt he buyes a Coach, that he may marry, and then hee marries that hee may be made Cuckold in't: For if their wiues ride not to their Cuckolding, they doe 'hem no credit. Hide, and be hidden; ride, and be ridden, ſayes the vapour of experience.

div.head: ACT. IIIJ.
SCENE. VI.
TROBLE-ALL. KNOCKHVM. VVHIT. QVARLOVS. EDGVVORTH. BRISTLE. WASPE. HAGGISE. IVSTICE. BVSY. PVRE-CRAFT.

sp.p: BY what warrant do's it ſay ſo?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Ha! mad child o'the Pye-pouldres, art thou there? fill vs a freſh kan, Vrſ, wee may drinke together.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: I may not drinke without a warrant, Captaine.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: S'lood, thou'll not ſtale without a warant, ſhortly. Whit, Giue mee pen, inke and paper. I'l draw him a warrant preſently.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: It muſt be Iuſtice Ouerdoo's?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: I know, man, Fetch the drinke, Whit.

sp.speaker: VVHI.
sp.p: I pre dee now, be very briefe, Captaine; for de new Ladies ſtay for dee.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: O, as briefe as can be, here 'tis already. Adam Ouerdoo.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: VVhy, now, I'le pledge you, Captaine.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Drinke it off. I'll come to thee, anone, againe.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Well, Sir. You are now diſcharg'd: Quarlous to the Cutpurſe. beware of being ſpi'd, hereafter.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p:
Sir, will it pleaſe you, enter in here, at Vrſla's; and take
part of a ſilken gowne, a veluet petticoate, or a wrought ſmocke; I am promiſ'd ſuch: and I can ſpare any Gentleman a moity.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Keepe it for your companions in beaſtlineſſe, I am none of'hem, Sir. If I had not already forgiuen you a greater treſpaſſe, or thought you yet worth my beating, I would inſtruct your manners, to whom you made your offers. But goe your wayes, talke not to me, the hangman is onely fit to diſcourſe with you; the hand of Beadle is too mercifull a puniſhment for your Trade of life. I am ſorry I employ'd this fellow; for he thinks me ſuch: Facinus quos inquinat, aequat. But, it was for ſport. And would I make it ſerious, the getting of this Licence is nothing to me, without other circumſtances concurre. I do thinke how impertinently I labour, if the word bee not mine, that the ragged fellow mark'd: And what aduantage I haue giuen Ned Win-wife in this time now, of working her, though it be mine. Hee'll go neare to forme to her what a debauch'd Raſcall I am, and fright her out of all good conceipt of me: I ſhould doe ſo by him, I am ſure, if I had the opportunity. But my hope is in her temper, yet; and it muſt needs bee next to deſpaire, that is grounded on any part of a woman's diſcretion. I would giue by my troth, now, all I could ſpare (to my cloathes, and my ſword) to meete my tatter'd ſooth-ſayer againe, who was my iudge i'rhe queſtion, to know certainly whoſe word he has damn'd or ſau'd. For, till then, I liue but vnder a Repreiue. I muſt ſeeke him. Who be theſe?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Ent Waſpe with the officers. Sir, you are a welſh Cuckold, and a prating Runt, and no Conſtable.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: You ſay very well. Come put in his legge in the middle roundell, and let him hole there.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: You ſtinke of leeks, Metheglyn, and cheeſe. You Rogue.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Why, what is that to you, if you ſit ſweetly in the ſtocks in the meane time? if you haue a minde to ſtinke too, your breeches ſit cloſe enough to your bumm. Sit you merry, Sir.

sp.speaker: QVA
sp.p: How now, Numps?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: It is no matter, how; pray you looke off.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Nay I'll not offend you, Numps. I thought you had ſate there to be ſeen.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: And to be ſold, did you not? pray you mind your buſineſſe, an' you haue any.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Cry you mercy, Numps. Do's your leg lie high enough?

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: How now, neighbour Haggiſe, what ſayes Iuſtice Ouerdo's worſhip, to the other offenders?

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: Why, hee ſayes iuſt nothing, what ſhould hee ſay? Or where ſhould he ſay? He is not to be found, Man. He ha' not been ſeen i'the Fayre, here, all this liue-long day, neuer ſince ſeuen a clocke i' the morning. His Clearks know not what to thinke on't. There is no Court of Pie-poulders yet. Heere they be return'd.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: What ſhall be done with 'hem, then? in your diſcretion?

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: I thinke wee were beſt put 'hem in the ſtocks, in diſcretion (there they will be ſafe in diſcretion) for the valour of an houre, or ſuch a thing, till his worſhip come. As they opén the ſtockes, Waſpe puts his ſhooe on his hand, and ſlips it in for his legge.

sp.speaker: BRI
sp.p: It is but a hole matter, if wee doe, Neighbour Haggiſe, come, Sir, heere is company for you, heaue vp the ſtocks.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I ſhall put a tricke vpon your welſh diligence, perhaps.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Put in your legge, Sir.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: What, Rabby Buſy! is hee come?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: I doe obey thee, the Lyon may roare, but he cannot bite. They bring Buſy, and put him in. I am glad to be thus ſeparated from the heathen of the land, and put a part in the ſtocks, for the holy cauſe.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: VVhat are you, Sir?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: One that reioyceth in his affliction, and ſitteth here to propheſie, the deſtruction of Fayres and May-games, Wakes, and Whitſon-ales, and doth ſigh and groane for the reformation, of theſe abuſes.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: And doe you ſigh, and groane too, or reioyce in your affliction?

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I doe not feele it, I doe not thinke of it, it is a thing without mee: Adam, thou art aboue theſe battries, theſe contumelies. In te manca ruit fortuna, as thy friend Horace ſaies; thou art one, Quem neque pauperies, neque mors, neque vincula terrent,. And therefore as another friend of thine ſaies, (I thinke it be thy friend Perſius) Non te quaeſiueris extra.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: What's heere! a Stoick i'the ſtocks? the Foole is turn'd Philoſopher.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Friend, I will leaue to communicate my ſpirit with you, if I heare any more of thoſe ſuperſtitious reliques, thoſe liſts of Latin, the very tags of Rome▪ and patches of Poperie.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Nay, an' you begin to quarrel, Gentlemen, I'll leaue you. I ha' paid for quarrelling too lately: looke you, a deuice, He get • out. but ſhifting in a hand for a foot. God b'w'you.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Wilt thou then leaue thy brethren in tribulation?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: For this once, Sir.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Thou art a halting Neutrall ſtay him there, ſtop him: that will not endure the heat of perſecution.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: How now, what's the matter?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Hee is fled, he is fled, and dares not ſit it out.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: What, has he made an eſcape, which way? follow, neighbour Haggiſe.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: O me! in the ſtocks! haue the wicked preuail'd?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Peace religious ſiſter, it is my calling, comfort your ſelfe, an extraordinary calling, and done for my better ſtanding, my ſurer ſtanding, hereafter.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: By whoſe warrant, by whoſe warrant, this?The mad-man enters.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: O, here's my man! dropt in, I look'd for.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Ha!

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: O good Sir, they haue ſet the faithfull, here to be wonder'd at; and prouided holes, for the holy of the land.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Had they warrant for it? ſhew'd they Iuſticce Ouerdoo's hand? if they had no warrant, they ſhall anſwer it.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Sure you did not locke the ſtocks ſufficiently, neighbour Toby!

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: No! ſee if you can lock 'hem better.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: They are very ſufficiently lock'd, and truely, yet ſome thing is in the mater.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: True, your warrant is the matter that is in queſtion, by what warrant?

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: Mad man, hold your peace, I will put you in his roome elſe, in the very ſame hole, doe you ſee?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: How! is hee a mad-man!

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Shew me Iuſtice Ouerdoo's warrant. I obey you.

sp.speaker: HAG.
sp.p: You are a mad foole, hold your tongue.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Shewes his Kanne. In Iuſtice Ouerdoo's name, I drinke to you, and here's my warrant.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Alas poore wretch! how it earnes my heart for him!

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: If hee be mad, it is in vaine to queſtion him. I'le try though, friend: there was a Gentlewoman, ſhew'd you two names, ſome houre ſince, Argalus and Palemon, to marke in a booke, which of 'hem was it you mark'd?

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: I marke no name, but Adam Ouerdoo, that is the name of names, hee onely is the ſufficient Magiſtrate; and that name I reuerence, ſhew it mee.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: This fellowes madde indeede: I am further off, now, then afore.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I ſhall not breath in peace, till I haue made him ſome amends.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Well, I will make another vſe of him, is come in my head: I haue a neſt of beards in my Truncke, one ſome thing like his.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: The watchmen come back againe. The mad-man fights with 'hem, and they leaue open the ſtocks. This mad foole has made mee that I know not whether I I haue lock'd the ſtocks or no, I thinke I lock'd 'hem.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Take Adam Ouerdoo in your minde, and feare nothing.

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: S'lid, madneſſe it ſelfe, hold thy peace, and take that.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Strikeſt thou without a warrant? take thou that.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Wee are deliuered by miracle; fellow in fetters, let vs not refuſe the meanes, this madneſſe was of the ſpirit: The malice of the enemy hath mock'd it ſelfe.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p:
Mad doe they call him! the world is mad in error, but hee is mad in truth: I loue him o'the ſudden, (the cunning man ſayd all true) and ſhall loue him more, and more. How well it becomes a man to be mad in truth! O, that I might be his yoake-fellow, and be mad with him, what a many ſhould wee draw to
madneſſe in truth, with vs!

sp.speaker: BRI.
sp.p: How now! all ſcap'd? where's the woman? it is witchcraft!The watch miſsing them are affrighted. Her veluet hat is a witch, o' my conſcience, or my key! t'one. The mad-man was a Diuell, and I am an Aſſe; ſo bleſſe me, my place, and mine office.

WEll, Lucke and Saint Bartholmew; out with the ſigne of our inuention, in the name of Wit, and do you beat the Drum, the while; All the fowle i'the Fayre, I meane, all the dirt in Smithfield, (that's one of Maſter Littlewit's Carwhitchets now) will be throwne at our Banner to day, if the matter do's not pleaſe the people. O the Motions, that I Lanthorne Leatherhead haue giuen light to, i' my time, ſince my Maſter Pod dyed! Ieruſalem was a ſtately thing; and ſo was Niniue, and the citty of Norwich, and Sodom and Gomorrah;
Pod was a Maſter of motions before him. with the riſing o'the prentiſes; and pulling downe the bawdy houſes there, vpon Shroue-Tueſday; but the Gunpowder-plot, there was a get-penny! I haue preſented that to an eighteene, or twenty pence audience, nine times in an afternoone. Your home-borne proiects proue euer the beſt, they are ſo eaſie, and familiar, they put too much learning i'their things now o'dayes: and that I feare will be the ſpoile o'this. Little-wit? I ſay, Mickle-wit! if not too mickle! looke to your gathering there, good man Filcher.

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: I warrant you, Sir.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: And there come any Gentlefolks, take two pence a piece, Sharkwell.

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.p: I warrant you, Sir, three pence, an'we can.

The Iuſtice comes in like a Porter. THis later diſguiſe, I haue borrow'd of a Porter, ſhall carry me out to all my great and good ends; which how euer interrupted, were neuer deſtroyed in me: neither is the houre of my ſeuerity yet come, to reueale my ſelfe, wherein cloud-like, I will breake out in raine, and haile, lightning, and thunder, vpon the head of enormity. Two maine works I haue to proſecute: firſt, one is to inuent ſome ſatisfaction for the poore, kinde wretch, who is out of his wits for my ſake, and yonder I ſee him comming, I will walke aſide, and proiect for it.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: I wonder where Tom Quarlous is, that hee returnes not, it may be he is ſtrucke in here to ſeeke vs.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: See, heere's our mad-man againe.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: I haue made my ſelfe as like him, as his gowne, and cap will giue me leaue. Quarlous in the habit of the mad-man is miſtaken by Mrs Pure-craft.

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Sir, I loue you, and would be glad to be mad with you in truth.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: How! my widdow in loue with a mad-man?

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Verily, I can be as mad in ſpirit, as you.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: By whoſe warrant? leaue your canting. Gentlewoman, haue I found you? (ſaue yee, quit yee, and multiply yee) where's your booke?He deſires to ſee the booke of Miſtreſſe Grace. 'twas a ſufficient name I mark'd, let me ſee't, be not afraid to ſhew't me.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: What would you with it, Sir?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Marke it againe, and againe, at your ſeruice.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Heere it is, Sir, this was it you mark'd.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Palemon? fare you well, fare you well.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: How, Palemon!

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Yes faith, hee has diſcouer'd it to you, now, and therefore 'twere vaine to diſguiſe it longer, I am yours, Sir, by the benefit of your fortune.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: And you haue him Miſtreſſe, beleeue it, that ſhall neuer giue you cauſe to repent her benefit, but make you rather to thinke that in this choyce, ſhe had both her eyes.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: I deſire to put it to no danger of proteſtation.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Palemon, the word, and Win-wife the man?

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Good Sir, vouchſafe a yoakefellow in your madneſſe, ſhun not one of the ſanctified ſiſters, that would draw with you, in truth.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Away, you are a heard of hypocriticall proud Ignorants, rather wilde, then mad. Fitter for woods, and the ſociety of beaſts th • n houſes, and the congregation of men. You are the ſecond part of the ſociety of Canters, Outlawes to order and Diſcipline, and the onely priuiledg'd Church-robbers of Chriſtendome. Let me alone. Palemon, the word, and Winwife the man?

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: I muſt vncouer my ſelfe vnto him, or I ſhall neuer enioy him, for all the cunning mens promiſes. Good Sir, heare mee, I am worth ſixe thouſand pound, my loue to you, is become my racke, I'll tell you all, and the truth: ſince you hate the hyporiſie of the party-coloured brother-hood. Theſe ſeuen yeeres, I haue beene a wilfull holy widdow, onely to draw feaſts, and gifts from my intangled ſuitors: I am alſo by office, an aſſiſting ſiſter of the Deacons, and a deuourer, in ſtead of a diſtributer of the alms. I am a ſpeciall maker of marriages for our decayed Brethren, with our rich widdowes; for a third part of their wealth, when they are marryed, for the reliefe of the poore elect: as alſo our poore handſome yong Virgins, with our wealthy Batchelors, or Widdowers; to make them ſteale from their husbands, when I haue confirmed them in the faith; and got all put into their cuſtodies. And if I ha' not my bargaine, they may ſooner turne a ſcolding drab, in to a ſilent Miniſter, then make me leaue pronouncing reprobation, and damnation vnto them. Our elder, Zeale-of-the-land, would haue had me, but I know him to be the capitall Knaue of the land, making himſelfe rich, by being made Feoffee in truſt to deceaſed Brethren, and coozning their heyres, by ſwearing the abſolute gift of their inheritance. And thus hauing eas'd my conſcience, and vtter'd my heart, with the tongue of my loue: enioy all my deceits together. I beſeech you. I ſhould not haue reuealed this to you, but that in time I thinke you are mad, and I hope you'll thinke mee ſo too, Sir?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Stand aſide, I'le anſwer you, preſently. He conſider with himſelfe of it. Why ſhould not I marry this ſixe thouſand pound, now I thinke on't? and a good trade too, that ſhee has beſide, ha? The tother wench, Winwife, is ſure of; there's no expectation for me there! here I may make my ſelfe ſome ſauer, yet, if ſhee continue mad, there's the queſtion. It is money that I want, why ſhould I not marry the money, when 'tis offer'd mee? I haue a Licenſe and all, it is but razing out one name, and putting in another. There's no playing with a man's fortune! I am reſolu'd! I were truly mad, an' I would not! well, come your wayes, follow mee, an' you will be mad, He takes her along with him. I'll ſhew you a warrant!

sp.speaker: PVR.
sp.p: Moſt zealouſly, it is that I zealouſly deſire.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Sir, let mee ſpeake with you. The Iuſtice calls him.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: By whoſe warrant?

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: The warrant that you tender, and reſpect ſo; Iuſtice Ouerdoo's! I am the man, friend Trouble-all, though thus diſguis'd (as the carefull Magiſtrate ought) for the good of the Republique, in the Fayre, and the weeding out of enormity. Doe you want a houſe or meat, or drinke, or cloathes? ſpeake whatſoeuer it is, it ſhall be ſupplyed you, what want you?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Nothing but your warrant.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: My warrant? for what?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: To be gone, Sir.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Nay, I pray thee ſtay, I am ſerious, and haue not many words, nor much time to exchange with thee; thinke what may doe thee good.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Your hand and ſeale, will doe me a great deale of good; nothing elſe in the whole Fayre, that I know.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: If it were to any end, thou ſhould'ſt haue it willingly.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Why, it will ſatisfie me, that's end enough, to looke on; an' you will not gi' it mee, let me goe.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Alas! thou ſhalt ha' it preſently▪ I'll but ſtep into the Scriueners, The Iuſtice goes out. hereby, and bring it. Doe not go away.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Why, this mad mans ſhape, will proue a very fortunate one, I thinke! can a ragged robe produce theſe effects? if this be the wiſe Iuſtice, and he bring mee his hand, I ſhall goe neere to make ſome vſe on't. and returns. Hee is come already!

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Looke thee! heere is my hand and ſeale, Adam Ouerdoo, if there be any thing to be written, aboue in the paper, that thou want'ſt now, or at any time hereafter; thinke on't; it is my deed, I deliuer it ſo, can your friend write?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Hee vrgeth Miſtreſſe Purecraft. Her hand for a witneſſe, and all is well.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: With all my heart.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Why ſhould not I ha' the conſcience, to make this a bond of a thouſand pound? now, or what I would elſe?

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Looke you, there it is; and I deliuer it as my deede againe.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Let vs now proceed in madneſſe.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: He takes her in with him. Well, my conſcience is much eas'd; I ha' done my part, though it doth him no good, yet Adam hath offer'd ſatisfaction! The ſting is remoued from hence: poore man, he is much alter'd with his affliction, it has brought him low! Now, for my other worke, reducing the young man (I haue follow'd ſo long in loue) from the brinke of his bane, to the center of ſafety. Here, or in ſome ſuch like vaine place, I ſhall be ſure to finde him. I will waite the good time.

div.head: ACT. V.
SCENE. IIJ.
COKES. SHAKRVVEL. IVSTICE. FILCHER. IOHN. LANTERNE.

sp.p: HOw now? what's here to doe? friend, art thou the Maſter of the Monuments?

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.p: 'Tis a Motion, an't pleaſe your worſhip,

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: My phantaſticall brother in Law, Maſter Bartholmew Cokes!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: A Motion, what's that?He reads the Bill. The ancient moderne hiſtory of Hero, and Leander, otherwiſe called The Touchſtone of true Loue, with as true a tryall of friendſhip, betweene Damon, and Pithias, two faithfull friends o'the Bankſide? pretty i'faith, what's the meaning on't? is't an Enterlude? or what is't?

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: Yes Sir, pleaſe you come neere, wee'll take your money within.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Backe with theſe children; The boyes o'the Fayre follow him. they doe ſo follow mee vp and downe.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: By your leaue, friend.

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: You muſt pay, Sir, an' you goe in.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Who, I? I perceiue thou know'ſt not mee: call the Maſter o'the Motion.

sp.speaker: SHA
sp.p: What, doe you not know the Author, fellow Filcher? you muſt take no money of him; he muſt come in gratis: Mr. Littlewit is a voluntary; he is the Author.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Peace, ſpeake not too lowd, I would not haue any notice taken, that I am the Author, till wee ſee how it paſſes.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Maſter Littlewit, how do'ſt thou?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Maſter Cokes! you are exceeding well met: what, in your doublet, and hoſe, without a cloake, or a hat?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I would I might neuer ſtirre, as I am an honeſt man, and by that fire; I haue loſt all i'the Fayre, and all my acquaintance too; did'ſt thou meet any body that I know, Maſter Littlewit? my man Numps, or my ſiſter Ouerdoo, or Miſtreſſe Grace? pray thee Maſter Littlewit, lend mee ſome money to ſee the Interlude, here. I'le pay thee againe, as I am a Gentleman. If thou'lt but carry mee home, I haue money enough there.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: O, Sir, you ſhall command it, what, will a crowne ſerue you?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I think it well, what do we pay for comming in, fellowes?

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: Two pence, Sir.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Two pence? there's twelue pence, friend; Nay, I am a Gallant, as ſimple as I looke now; if you ſee mee with my man about me, and my Artillery, againe.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Your man was i'the Stocks, ee'n now, Sir.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Who, Numps?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Yes faith.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: For what i' faith, I am glad o' that; remember to tell me on't anone; I haue enough, now! What manner of matter is this, Mr. Littlewit? What kind of Actors ha' you? Are they good Actors?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Pretty youthes, Sir, all children both old and yong, heer's the Maſter of'hem—

sp.speaker: (LAN.
sp.p: Leatherhead whiſpers to Littlwit. Call me not Leatherhead, but Lanterne.)

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Maſter Lanterne, that giues light to the buſineſſe,

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: In good time, Sir, I would faine ſee 'hem, I would be glad drinke with the young company; which is the Tiring-houſe?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Troth, Sir, our Tiring-houſe is ſomewhat little, we are but beginners, yet, pray pardon vs; you cannot goe vpright in't.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: No? not now my hat is off? what would you haue done with me, if you had had me, feather, and all, as I was once to day? Ha' you none of your pretty impudent boyes, now; to bring ſtooles, fill Tabacco, fetch Ale, and beg money, as they haue at other houſes? let me ſee ſome o'your Actors.

sp.speaker: ION.
sp.p: Shew him 'hem, ſhew him 'hem. Maſter Lanterne, this is a Gentleman, that is a fauorer of the quality.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I, the fauouring of this licencious quality, is the conſumption of many a young Gentleman; a pernicious enormity.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: What, doe they liue in baskets?

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: He brings them out in a basket. They doe lye in a basket, Sir, they are o'the ſmall Players.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Theſe be Players minors, indeed. Doe you call theſe Players?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: They are Actors, Sir, and as good as any, none diſprais'd, for dumb ſhowes: indeed, I am the mouth of'hem all!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Thy mouth will hold 'hem all. I thinke, one Taylor, would goe neere to beat all this company, with a hand bound behinde him.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: I, and eate 'hem all, too, an' they were in cake-bread.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I thanke you for that, Maſter Littlewit, a good ieſt! which is your Burbage now?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: What meane you by that, Sir?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Your beſt Actor. Your Field?

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Good ifaith! you are euen with me, Sir.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p:
This is he, that acts young Leander, Sir. He is extreamly belou'd of the womenkind, they doe ſo affect his action, the
green gameſters, that come here, and this is louely Hero; this with the beard, Damon; and this pretty Pythias: this is the ghoſt of King Dionyſius in the habit of a ſcriuener: as you ſhall ſee anone, at large.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well they are a ciuill company, I like 'hem for that; they offer not to fleere, nor geere, nor breake ieſts, as the great Players doe: And then, there goes not ſo much charge to the feaſting of'hem, or making 'hem drunke, as to the other, by reaſon of their littleneſſe. Doe they vſe to play perfect? Are they neuer fluſter'd?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: No, Sir, I thanke my induſtry, and policy for it; they are as well gouern'd a company, though I ſay it—And here is young Leander, is as proper an Actor of his inches; and ſhakes his head like an hoſtler·

sp.speaker: COK
sp.p: But doe you play it according to the printed booke? I haue read that.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: By no meanes, Sir.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: No? How then?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: A better way, Sir, that is too learned, and poeticall for our audience; what doe they know what Helleſpont is? Guilty of true loues blood? or what Abidos is? or the other Seſtos height?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Th'art i'the right, I doe not know my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: No, I haue entreated Maſter Littlewit, to take a little paines to reduce it to a more familiar ſtraine for our people.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: How, I pray thee, good Mr Littlewit.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: It pleaſes him to make a matter of it, Sir. But there is no ſuch matter I aſſure you: I haue onely made it a little eaſie, and moderne for the times, Sir, that's all; As, for the Helleſpont I imagine our Thames here; and then Leander, I make a Diers ſonne, about Puddle-wharfe: and Hero a wench o' the Banke-ſide, who going ouer one morning, to old fiſh-ſtreet; Leander ſpies her land at Trigſstayres, and falls in loue with her: Now do I introduce Cupid, hauing Metamorphos'd himſelfe into a Drawer, and he ſtrikes Hero in loue with a pint of Sherry, and other pretty paſſages there are, o' the friendſhip, that will delight you, Sir, and pleaſe you of Iudgement.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I'll be ſworne they ſhall; I am in loue with the Actors already, and I'le be allyed to them preſently. (They reſpect gentlemen, theſe fellowes) Hero ſhall be my fayring: But, which of my fayrin • s? (Le' me ſee) i' faith, my fiddle! and Leander my fiddle-ſticke: Then Damon, my Drum; and Pythias, my Pipe and the ghoſt of Dionyſius, my hobby-horſe. All fitted.

div.head: ACT. V.
SCENE. IV.
To them WIN-WIFE. GRACE. KNOCKHVM. WHITT. EDGVVORTH. VVIN. Miſtris OVERDOO. And to them VVASPE.

sp.p: Looke yonder's your Cokes gotten in among his play-fellowes; I thought we could not miſſe him, at ſuch a Spectacle.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Let him alone, he is ſo buſie, he will neuer ſpie vs.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.p: Nay, good Sir.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Cokes is handling the Puppets. I warrant thee, I will not hurt her, fellow; what doſt think me vnciuill? I pray thee be not iealous: I am toward a wife.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: Well good Maſter Lanterne, make ready to begin, that I may fetch my wife, and looke you be perfect, you vndoe me elſe, i'my reputation.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: I warrant you Sir, doe not you breed too great an expectation of it, among your friends: that's the onely hurter of theſe things.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: No, no, no.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I'll ſtay here, and ſee; pray thee let me ſee.

sp.speaker: WIN-VV.
sp.p: How diligent and troubleſome he is!

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: The place becomes him, me thinkes.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: My ward, Miſtreſſe Grace in the company of a ſtranger? I doubt I ſhall be compell'd to diſcouer my ſelfe, before my time!

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: The doorekeepers ſpeake. Two pence a piece Gentlemen, an excellent Motion.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Shall we haue fine fire-works, and good vapours!

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.p: Yes Captaine, and water-works, too.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: I pree dee, take a care o'dy ſhmall Lady, there, Edgworth; I will looke to diſh tall Lady my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Welcome Gentlemen, welcome Gentlemen.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Predee, Maſhter o'de Monſhterſh, helpe a very ſicke Lady, here, to a chayre, to ſhit in.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Preſently, Sir.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: They bring Miſtris Ouerdoo a chayre. Good fait now, Vrſla's Ale, and Aqua-vitae iſh to blame for't; ſhit downe ſhweet heart, ſhit downe, and ſhleep a little.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Madame, you are very welcom hither.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Yes, and you ſhall ſee very good vapours.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Here is my care come! I like to ſee him in ſo good company; By Edgeworth. and yet I wonder that perſons of ſuch faſhion, ſhould reſort hither!

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: This is a very priuate houſe, Madame. The Cutpurſe courts Miſtreſſe Littlewit.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Will it pleaſe your Ladiſhip ſit, Madame?

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Yes good-man. They doe ſo all to be Madame mee, I thinke they thinke me a very Lady!

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: What elſe Madame?

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Muſt I put off my maſque to him?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: O, by no meanes.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: How ſhould my husband know mee, then?

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Husband? an idle vapour; he muſt not know you, nor you him; there's the true vapour.

sp.speaker: IVS
sp.p: Yea, I will obſerue more of this: is this a Lady, friend?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: I, and dat is anoder Lady, ſhweet heart; if dou haſht a minde to 'hem giue me twelue pence from tee, and dou ſhalt haue oder-oder on 'hem!

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I? This will prooue my chiefeſt enormity: I will follow this.

sp.speaker: EDG,
sp.p: Is not this a finer life, Lady, then to be clogg'd with a husband?

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Yes, a great deale. When will they beginne, trow? in the name o'the Motion?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: By and by Madame, they ſtay but for company.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Doe you heare, Puppet. Maſter, theſe are tedious vapours; when begin you?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: We ſtay but for Maſter Littlewit, the Author, who is gone for his wife; and we begin preſently.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: That's I, that's I.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: That was you, Lady; but now you are no ſuch poore thing.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Hang the Authors wife, a running vapour! here be Ladies, will ſtay for nere a Delia o'hem all.

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: But heare mee now, heere iſh one o'de Ladiſh, a ſhleep, ſtay till ſhee but vake man.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: How now friends? what's heere to doe?

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: Two pence a piece, Sir, the beſt Motion, in the Fayre. The doorekeepers againe.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I beleeue you lye; if you doe, I'll haue my money againe, and beat you.

sp.speaker: WIN.
sp.p: Numps is come!

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Did you ſee a Maſter of mine, come in here, a tall yong Squire of Harrow o'the Hill; Maſter Bartholmew Cokes?

sp.speaker: FIL.
sp.p: I thinke there be ſuch a one, within.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Looke hee be, you were beſt: but it is very likely: I wonder I found him not at all the reſt. I ha' beene at the Eagle, and the blacke Wolfe, and the Bull with the fiue legges, and two pizzles; (hee was a Calfe at Vxbridge Fayre, two yeeres agone) And at the dogges that daunce the Morrice, and the Hare o' the Taber; and miſt him at all theſe! Sure this muſt needs be ſome fine ſight, that holds him ſo, if it haue him.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Come, come, are you readie now?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Preſently, Sir.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Hoyday, hee's at worke in his Dublet, and hoſe; doe you heare, Sir? are you imploy'd? that you are bare-headed, and ſo buſie?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Hold your peace, Numpes; you ha' beene i'the ſtocks, I heare.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Do's he know that? nay, then the date of my Authority is out; I muſt thinke no longer to raigne, my gouernment is at an end. He that will correct another, muſt want fault himſelfe.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Sententious Numpes! I neuer heard ſo much from him, before.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Sure, Maſter Littlewit will not come; pleaſe you take your place, Sir, wee'll beginne.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I pray thee doe, mine eares long to be at it; and my eyes too. O Numpes, i'the ſtocks, Numps? where's your ſword, Numps?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I pray intend your game, Sir, let mee alone.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well, then we are quit for all. Come, ſit downe, Numps; I'le interpret to thee: did you ſee Miſtreſſe Grace? it's no matter, neither, now, I thinke on't, tell me anon.

sp.speaker: WIN-VV.
sp.p: A great deale of loue, and care hee expreſſes.

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: Alas! would you haue him expreſſe more then hee has? that were tyranny.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Peace, ho; now, now.

sp.speaker: LAN.

l.hi: Gentles, that no longer your expectations may wander,

sp.l: Behold our chiefe Actor, amorous Leander.

l.hi: With a great deale of cloth lap'd about him like a Scarfe,

sp.l: For he yet ſerues his father, a Dyer at Puddle wharfe,
VVhich place wee'll make bold with, to call our Abidus,
As the Banke-ſide is our Seſtos, and let it not be deny'd vs.

l.hi: Now, as hee is beating, to make the Dye take the fuller,

sp.l: Who chances to come by, but faire Hero, in a Sculler;
And ſeeing Leanders naked legge, and goodly calfe,

l.hi: Caſt at him, from the boat, a Sheepes eye, and a halfe.
Now ſhe is landed, and the Sculler come backe;

sp.l: By and by, you ſhall ſee what Leander doth lacke.

p.hi: That's the Scullers name without controle:

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Wee doe heare you.

p.hi: Old Cole? is the Dyer turn'd Collier? how doe you ſell?
A pox o' you manners, kiſſe my hole here and ſmell.
Kiſſe your hole and ſmell? there's manners indeed.

sp.speaker: LAN.

l.hi: Be hang'd; looke you yonder,

sp.l: Old Cole, you muſt go hang with Maſter Leander.

sp.speaker: PVP. L.

l.hi: Here, Cole, what fayereſt of Fayers,

sp.l: was that fare, that thou landedſt but now a Trigsſtayres?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: What was that, fellow? Pray thee tell me, I ſcarſe vnderſtand 'hem.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: Leander do's aske, Sir, what fayreſt of Fayers,
Was the fare thhe landed, but now, at Trigsſtayers?

sp.speaker: PVP. C.
sp.p: It is lo • ely Hero.

sp.speaker: PVP. L.
sp.p: Nero?

sp.speaker: PVP. C.
sp.p: No, Hero.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: It is Hero.

l.hi: Of the Bankſide, he ſaith, to tell you truthwith out erring,
Is come ouer into Fiſh-ſtreet to eat ſome freſh herring.

sp.l: Leander ſayes no more, but as faſt as he can,
Gets on all his beſt cloathes; and will after to the Swan.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Moſt admirable good, is't not?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Stay, Sculler.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: You muſt ſtay for Leander,

l.hi: and carry him to the wench.

p.hi: You Rogue, I am no Pandar.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: He ſayes he is no Pandar. 'Tis a fine language; I vnderſtand it, now.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: Are you no Pandar, Goodman Cole? heer's no man ſayes you are,

l.hi: You'll grow a hot Cole, it ſeemes, pray you ſtay for your fare.

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.l: VVould you ha' Leander come away? why 'pray' Sir, ſtay.
You are angry, Goodman Cole; I beleeue the faire Mayd

l.hi: Came ouer w' you a' truſt: tell vs Sculler, are you paid.

p.hi: I Hogrubber o' Pickt-hatch. Take you that.

p.stage: The Puppet ſtrikes him ouer the pate

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Harme watch, harme catch, he ſayes: verygood i' faith, the Sculler had like to ha' knock'd you, ſirrah.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Yes, but that his fare call'd him away.

p.hi: Row apace, row apace, row, row, row, row, row.
You are knauiſhly loaden, Sculler, take heed where you goe.
Knaue i' your face, Goodman Rogue.
Row, row, row, row, row, row.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Hee ſaid knaue i' your face, friend.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: I Sir, I heard him. But there's no talking to theſe water-men, they will ha' the laſt word

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: God's my life! I am not allied to the Sculler, yet; hee ſhall be Dauphin my boy. But my Fiddle-ſticke do's fiddle in and out too much; I pray thee ſpeake to him, on't: tell him, I would haue him tarry in my ſight, more.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p:
I Pray you be content; you'll haue enough on him, Sir.
Now gentles, I take it, here is none of you ſo ſtupid,
but that you haue heard of a little god of loue, call'd Cupid.
VVho out of kindnes to Leander, hearing he but ſaw her,
this preſent day and houre, doth turne himſelfe to a Drawer.
And becauſe, he would haue their firſt meeting to be merry,
he ſtrikes Hero in loue to him, with a pint of Sherry.
PVP. Leander goes into Miſtris Hero's room VVhich he tells her, from amorous Leander is ſent her,
who after him, into the roome of Hero, doth venter.

sp.speaker: PVP. Io:
sp.p: A pint of ſacke, ſcore a pint of ſacke, i' the Conney.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Sack? you ſaid but ee'n now it ſhould be Sherry.

p.hi: Why ſo it is; ſherry, ſherry, ſherry.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Sherry, ſherry, ſherry. By my troth he makes me merry. I muſt haue a name for Cupid, too. Let me ſee, thou mightſt helpe me now, an' thou wouldeſt, Numps, at a dead lift, but thou art dreaming o' the ſtocks, ſtill! Do not thinke on't, I haue forgot it: 'tis but a nine dayes wonder, man; let it not trouble thee.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I would the ſtocks were about your necke, Sir; condition I hung by the heeles in them, till the wonder were off from you, with all my heart.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well ſaid reſolute Numps: but hearke you friend, where is the friendſhip, all this while, betweene my Drum, Damon; and my Pipe, Pythias?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: You ſhall ſee by and by, Sir?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: You thinke my Hobby-horſe is forgotten, too; no, I'll ſee 'hem all enact before I go; I ſhall not know which to loue beſt, elſe

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: This Gallant has interrupting vapours, troubleſome vapours, Whitt, puffe with him.

sp.speaker: WHIT.
sp.p: No, I pre dee, Captaine, let him alone. Hee is a Child i' faith, la'.

sp.speaker: LAN.

l.hi: Now gentles, to the freinds, who in number, are two,

sp.l: and lodg'd in that Ale-houſe, in which faire Hero do's doe.
Damon ('for ſome kindneſſe done him the laſt weeke)
is come faire Hero, in Fiſh-ſtreete, this morning to ſeeke:
Pythias do's ſmell the knauery of the meeting,

l.hi: and now you ſhall ſee their true friendly greeting.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Whore-maſterly ſlaue, you? very friendly, & familiar, that.

l.hi: Whore-maſter i' thy face,
Thou haſt lien with her thy ſelfe, I'll proue't i' this place.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Damon ſayes Pythias has lien with her, himſelfe, hee'll prooue't in this place.

p.hi: They are Whore-maſters both, Sir, that's a plaine caſe.
Doe I ly, like a Rogue?

l.hi: A Pimpe, and a Scabbe?
I ſay between you, you haue both but one Drabbe.

p.hi: And you are a Pimpe, againe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: And you are a Pimpe againe, he ſayes.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: And a Scabbe againe, he ſayes.

l.hi: And I ſay againe, you are both whore-maſters againe,
and you haue both but one Drabbe againe.

p.hi: Do'ſt thou, do'ſt thou, do'ſt thou?

sp.speaker: PVP. P.
sp.p: Downe with him, Damon

sp.speaker: PVP. D.
sp.p: Pinke his guts, Pythias:

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: What, ſo malicious?

l.hi: will ye murder me, Maſters both, i' mine owne houſe?

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Ho! well acted my Drum, well acted my Pipe, well acted ſtill.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Well acted, with all my heart.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: I, both your hands, for my ſake! for you ha' both donewell.

sp.speaker: PVP. D.
sp.p: Gramercy pure Pythias.

sp.speaker: PVP. P.
sp.p: Gramercy, Deare Damon.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Gramercy to you both, my Pipe, and my drum.

sp.speaker: PVP. P. D.
sp.p: Come now wee'll together to breakfaſt to Hero.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: 'Tis well, you can now go to breakfaſt to Hero,
you haue giuen mmy breakfaſt, with a hone and honero.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: How is't friend, ha' they hurt thee?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: O no!
Betweene you and I Sir, we doe but make ſhow.

q.l: Thus Gentles you perceiue, without any deniall,
'twixt Damon and Pythias here, friendſhips true tryall.
Though hourely they quarrell thus, and roare each with other,
they fight you no more, then do's brother with brother.
But friendly together, at the next man they meet,
they let fly their anger as here you might ſee't.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well, we haue ſeen't, and thou haſt felt it, whatſoeuer thou ſayeſt, what's next? what's next?

sp.speaker: LEA.
sp.l: This while young Leander, with faire Hero is drinking,
and Hero growne drunke, to any mans thinking!

l.hi: Yet was it not three pints of Sherry could flaw her.

sp.l: till Cupid diſtinguiſh'd like Ionas the Drawer,

l.hi: From vnder his apron, where his lechery lurkes,
put loue in her Sacke. Now marke how it workes.

sp.speaker: PVP. H.
sp.l: O Leander Leander, my deare my deare Leander,

l.hi: I'le for euer be thy gooſe, ſo thou'lt be my gander▪

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Excellently well ſaid, Fiddle, ſhee'll euer be his gooſe, ſo hee'll be her gander: was't not ſo?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Yes, Sir, but marke his anſwer, now:

sp.speaker: PVP. L.

l.hi: And ſweeteſt of geeſe, before I goe to bed,

sp.l: I'll ſwimme o're the Thames, my gooſe, thee to tread.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Braue! he will ſwimme o're the Thames, and tread his gooſe, too night, he ſayes.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: I, peace, Sir, the'll be angry, if they heare you eaueſ-dropping, now they are ſetting their match.

l.hi: But leſt the Thames ſhould be dark, my gooſe, my deare friend,
let thy window be prouided of a candles end.
Feare not my gander, I proteſt, I ſhould handle
my matters very ill, if I had not a whole candle.

p.hi: Well then, looke to't, and kiſſe me to boote.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: Now, heere come the friends againe, Pythias, aend Damon,

l.hi: and vnder their clokes, they haue of Bacon, a gammon.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.l: How, ſome wine there?
there's company already, Sir, pray forbeare!

sp.speaker: PVP. D.
sp.p: 'Tis Hero.

sp.speaker: LAN.

l.hi: Yes, but ſhee will not be taken,

sp.l: after • acke, and freſh herring, with your Dunmow-bacon.

sp.speaker: PVP. P
sp.p: You lye, it's Weſtfabian.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Westphalian you ſhould ſay.

p.hi: If you hold not your peace, you are a Coxcombe, I would ſay.
What's here? what's here? kiſſe, kiſſe, vpon kiſſe.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: I, Wherefore ſhould they not? what harme is in this? 'tis Miſtreſſe Hero.

sp.speaker: PVP. D.
sp.p: Miſtreſſe Hero's a whore.

p.hi: Is ſhee a whore? keepe you quiet, or Sir Knaue out of dore.

p.stage: Heere the Puppets quarrell and fall together by the eares.

p.hi: I ſay, Knaue, out of doore.
I ſay, whore, out of doore.

l.hi: Now you ha' ſomething to doe:
you muſt kiſſe her o' the arſe ſhee ſayes:

p.hi: So we will, ſo we will.
O my hanches, O my hanches, hold, hold.

sp.speaker: LAN.

l.hi: Stand'ſt thou ſtill?

sp.l: Leander, where art thou? ſtand'ſt thou ſtill like a ſot,

l.hi: and not offer'ſt to breake both their heads with a pot?

sp.l: See who's at thine elbow, there! Puppet Ionas and Cupid.

sp.speaker: PVP. I.
sp.p: Vpon'hem Leander, be not ſo ſtupid. They fight.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: See, Cupid with a word has tane vp the brawle.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Theſe be fine vapours!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: By this good day they fight brauely! doe they not, Numps?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Yes, they lack'd but you to be their ſecond, all this while.

sp.speaker: LAN.

l.hi: This tragicall encounter, falling out thus to buſie vs,

sp.l: It raiſes vp the ghoſt of their friend Dionyſius:

l.hi: Not like a Monarch, but the Maſter of a Schoole,
in a Scriueners furr'd gowne, which ſhewes he is no foole.
for therein he hath wit enough to keepe himſelfe warme.

sp.l: O Damon he cries, and Pythias; what harme,
Hath poore Dionyſius done you in his graue,

l.hi: That after his death, you ſhould fall out thus, and raue,

sp.l: And call amorous Leander whore-maſter Knaue?

p.hi: I cannot, I will not, I promiſe you endure it.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Downe with Dagon, downe with Dagon; 'tis I, will no longer endure your prophanations.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: What meane you, Sir?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: I wil remoue Dagon there, I ſay, that Idoll, that heatheniſh Idoll, that remaines (as I may ſay) a beame, a very beame, not a beame of the Sunne, nor a beame of the Moone, nor a beame of a ballance, neither a houſe-beame, nor a Weauers beame, but a beame in the eye, in the eye of the brethren; a very great beame, an exceeding great beame; ſuch as are your Stage-players, Rimers, and Morriſe-dancers, who haue walked hand in hand, in contempt of the Brethren, and the Cauſe; and beene borne out by inſtruments, of no meane countenance.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Sir, I preſent nothing, but what is licens'd by authority.

sp.speaker: BAS.
sp.p: Thou art all licenſe, euen licentiouſneſſe it ſelfe, Shimei!

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: I haue the Maſter of the Reuell's haud for't, Sir.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: The Maſter of Rebells hand, thou haſt; Satan's! hold thy peace, thy ſcurrility ſhut vp thy mouth, thy profeſſion is damnable, and in pleading for it, thou doſt plead for Baal. I haue long opened my mouth wide, and gaped, I haue gaped as the oyſter for the tide, after thy deſtruction: but cannot compaſſe it by ſute, or diſpute; ſo that I looke for a bickering, ere long, and then a battell.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Good Banbury-vapours.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Friend, you'ld haue an ill match on't, if you bicker with him here, though he be no man o'the fiſt, hee has friends that will goe to cuffes for him, Numps, will not you take our ſide?

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Sir, it ſhall not need, in my minde, he offers him a fairer courſe, to end it by diſputation! haſt thou nothing to ſay for thy ſelfe, in defence of thy quality?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Faith, Sir, I am not well ſtudied in theſe controuerſies, betweene the hypocrites and vs. But here's one of my Motion, Puppet Doniſius ſhall vndertake him, and I'le venture the cauſe on't.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Who? my Hobby-horſe? will he diſpute with him?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Yes, Sir, and make a Hobby-Aſſe of him, I hope.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: That's excellent! indeed he lookes like the beſt ſcholler of'hem all. Come, Sir, you muſt be as good as your word, now.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: I will not feare to make my ſpirit, and gifts knowne! aſſiſt me zeale, fill me, fill me, hat is, make me full.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: What a deſperate, prophane wretch is this! is there any Ignorance, or impudence like his? to call his zeale to fill him againſt a Puppet?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: I know no fitter match, then a Puppet to commit with an Hypocrite!

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Firſt, I ſay vnto thee, Idoll, thou haſt no Calling.

sp.speaker: PVP. D.
sp.p: You lie, I am call'd Dioniſius.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: The Motion ſayes you lie, he is call'd Dioniſius ithe matter, and to that calling he anſwers.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: I meane no vocation, Idoll, no preſent lawfull Calling.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: The Motion asketh, if yours be a lawfull Calling?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Yes, mine is of the Spirit.

sp.speaker: PVP. D.
sp.p: Then Idoll is a lawfull Calling.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: He ſaies, then Idoll is a lawfull Calling! for you call'd him Idoll, and your Calling is of the ſpirit.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Well diſputed, Hobby-horſe!

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Take not part with the wicked young Gallant. He neygheth and hinneyeth, all is but hinnying Sophiſtry. I call him Idoll againe. Yet, I ſay, his Calling, his Profeſſion is prophane, it is prophane, Idoll.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: It is not prophane, he ſayes▪

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: It is prophane.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: It is prophane.

sp.speaker: LAN
sp.p: Well ſaid, confute him with not, ſtill. You cannot beare him downe with your baſe noyſe, Sir.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Nor he me, with his treble creeking, though he creeke like the chariot wheeles of Satan; I am zealous for the Cauſe—

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: As a dog for a bone.

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: And I ſay, it is prophane, as being the Page of Pride, and the waiting woman of vanity.

p.hi: Yea? what ſay you to your Tire-women, then?

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Good.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: Or feather-makers i' the Fryers, that are o' your faction of faith? Are not they with their perrukes, and their puffes, their fannes, and their huffes, as much Pages of Pride, and waiters vpon vanity? what ſay you? what ſay you? what ſay you?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: I will not anſwer for them.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: Becauſe you cannot, becauſe you cannot. Is a Bugle-maker a lawfull Calling? or the Confect-makers? ſuch you haue there: or your French Faſhioner? you'ld haue all the ſinne within your ſelues, would you not? would you not?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: No, Dagon.

p.hi: What then, Dagonet? is a Puppet worſe then theſe?

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Yes, and my maine argument againſt you, is, that you are an abomination: for the Male, among you, putteth on the apparell of the Female, and the Female of the Male.

p.hi: You lye, you lye, you lye abominably.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Good, by my troth, he has giuen him the lye thrice.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: It is your old ſtale argument againſt the Players, but it will not hold againſt the Puppets; for we haue neyther Male nor Female amongst vs. And that thou may'ſt ſee, if thou wilt, The Puppet takes vp his garment. like a malicious purblinde zeale as thou art!

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: By my faith, there he has anſwer'd you, friend; by playne demonſtration.

sp.speaker: PVP.
sp.p: Nay, I'le proue, againſt ere a Rabbin of'hem all, that my ſtanding is as lawfull as his; that I ſpeak by inſpiration, as well as he; that I haue as little to doe with learning as he; and doe ſcorne her helps as much as he.

sp.speaker: BVS,
sp.p: I am confuted, the Cauſe hath failed me.

sp.speaker: LAN.
sp.p: Be conuerted, I pray you, and let the Play goe on!

sp.speaker: BVS.
sp.p: Let it goe on. For I am changed, and will become a beholder with you!

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: That's braue i'faith, thou haſt carryed it away, Hobby-horſe, on with the Play!

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Stay, now do I forbid, I Adam Ouerdoo! ſit ſtill, I charge you. The Iuſtice diſcouers himſelfe.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: What, my Brother i'law!

sp.speaker: GRA.
sp.p: My wiſe Guardian!

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: It is time, to take Enormity by the fore head, and brand it; for, I haue diſcouer'd enough.

div.head: ACT. V.
SCENE. VI.
To them, QVARLOVS. (like the Mad-man) PVRECRAFT. (a while after) IOHN. to them TROVBLE-ALL. VRSLA. NIGHTIGALE.

sp.speaker: QVAR.
sp.p: Nay, come Miſtreſſe Bride. You muſt doe as I doe, now. You muſt be mad with mee, in truth. I haue heere Iustice Ouerdoo for it.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Peace good Trouble-all; come hither, and you ſhall trouble none. To the Cutpurſe, and Miſtreſſe Litwit. I will take the charge of you, and your friend too, you alſo, young man ſhall be my care, ſtand there.

sp.speaker: EDG.
sp.p: Now, mercy vpon mee.

sp.speaker: KNO.
sp.p: Would we were away, Whit, theſe are dangerous vapours, The reſt are ſtealing away. beſt fall off with our birds, for feare o'the Cage.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Stay, is not my name your terror?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Yeſh faith man, and it iſh fot tat, we would be gone man.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: O Gentlemen! did you not ſee a wife of mine? I ha' loſt my little wife, as I ſhall be truſted: my little pretty Win, I left her at the great woman's houſe in truſt yonder, the Pig-womans, with Captaine Iordan, and Captaine Whit, very good men, and I cannot heare of her. Poore foole, I feare ſhee's ſtepp'd aſide. Mother, did you not ſee Win?

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: If this graue Matron be your mother, Sir, ſtand by her, Et digito compeſce labellum, I may perhaps ſpring a wife for you, anone. Brother Bartholmew, I am ſadly ſorry, to ſee you ſo lightly giuen, and ſuch a Diſciple of enormity: with your graue Gouernour Humphrey: but ſtand you both there, in the middle place; I will reprehend you in your courſe. Miſtreſſe Grace, let me reſcue you out of the hands of the ſtranger.

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Pardon me, Sir, I am a kinſman of hers.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Are you ſo? of what name, Sir?

sp.speaker: WIN-W.
sp.p: Winwife, Sir:

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p:
Maſter Winwife? I hope you haue won no wife of her, Sir. If you haue, I will examine the poſſibility of it, at fit leaſure. Now, to my enormities: looke vpon mee, O London! and ſee mee, O Smithfield; The example of Iuſtice, and Mirror of Magiſtrates: the true top of formality, and ſcourge of enormity. Harken vnto my
labours, and but obſerue my diſcoueries; and compare Hercules with me, if thou dar'ſt, of old; or Columbus; Magellan; or our countrey man Drake of later times: ſtand forth you weedes of enormity, and ſpread. Firſt, Rabbi Buſy, thou ſuperlunaticall hypocrite, next, To Buſy, To Lantern, To the horſe courſer, and Cutpurſe. Then Cap. Whit, and Miſtreſſe Littlewit. thou other extremity, thou prophane profeſſor of Puppetry, little better then Poetry: then thou ſtrong Debaucher, and Seducer of youth; witneſſe this eaſie and honeſt young man: now thou Eſquire of Dames, Madams, and twelue-penny Ladies: now my greene Madame her ſelfe, of the price. Let mee vnmaſque your Ladiſhip.

sp.speaker: IOH.
sp.p: O my wife, my wife, my wife!

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Is ſhe your wife? Redde te Harpocratem!

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: By your leaue, ſtand by my Maſters, be vncouer'd.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: O ſtay him, ſtay him, helpe to cry, Nightingale; my pan, Enter Trouble-all. my panne.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: What's the matter?

sp.speaker: NIG.
sp.p: Hee has ſtolne gammar Vrſla's panne.

sp.speaker: TRO.
sp.p: Yes, and I feare no man but Iuſtice Ouerdoo.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Vrſla? where is ſhe? O the Sow of enormity, this! To Vrſla, and Nightingale. welcome, ſtand you there, you Songſter, there.

sp.speaker: VRS.
sp.p: An' pleaſe your worſhip, I'am in no fault: A Gentleman ſtripp'd him in my Booth, and borrow'd his gown, and his hat; and hee ranne away with my goods, here, for it.

sp.speaker: IVS·
sp.p: Then this is the true mad-man, and you are the enormity! To Quarlous.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: You are i'the right, I am mad, but from the gowne outward.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Stand you there.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Where you pleaſe, Sir.

sp.speaker: OVER
sp.p: O lend me a baſon, I am ſicke, I am ſicke; Miſtreſſe Ouerdoo is ſicke: and her husband is ſilenc'd. where's Mr. Ouerdoo? Bridget, call hither my Adam.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: How?

sp.speaker: WHI.
sp.p: Dy very owne wife, i'fait, worſhipfull Adam.

sp.speaker: OVER.
sp.p: Will not my Adam come at mee? ſhall I ſee him no more then?

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p:
Sir, why doe you not goe on with the enormity? are you oppreſt with it? I'le helpe you: harke you Sir, i'your eare, your Innocent young man, you haue tane ſuch care of, all this day, is a Cutpurſe; that hath got all your brother Cokes his things, and help'd you to your beating, and the ſtocks; if you haue a minde to hang him now, and ſhew him your Magiſtrates wit, you may: but I ſhould think it were better, recouering the goods, and to ſaue your eſtimation in him. I thank you Sr. for the gift of your Ward, Mrs. Grace: look you, here is your hand & ſeale, by the way. Mr. Win-wife giue you ioy, you are Palemon, you are poſſeſt o'the Gentlewoman, but ſhe muſt pay me value, here's warrant for it. And honeſt mad-man, there's thy gowne, and cap againe; I thanke thee for my wife. To the widdow. Nay, I can be mad, ſweet heart, when I pleaſe, ſtill; neuer feare me:
And carefull Numps, where's he? I thanke him for my licence.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: Waſpe miſſeth the Licence. How!

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: 'Tis true, Numps.

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I'll be hang'd then.

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: Loke i'your boxe, Numps, nay, Sir, ſtand not you fixt here, like a ſtake in Finsbury to be ſhot at, or the whipping poſt i'the Fayre, but get your wife out o'the ayre, it wil make her worſe elſe; and remember you are but Adam, Fleſh, and blood! you haue your frailty, forget your other name of Ouerdoo, and inuite vs all to ſupper. There you and I will compare our diſcoueries; and drowne the memory of all enormity in your bigg'ſt bowle at home.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: How now, Numps, ha' you loſt it? I warrant, 'twas when thou wert i'the ſtocks: why doſt not ſpeake?

sp.speaker: WAS.
sp.p: I will neuer ſpeak while I liue, againe, for ought I know.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: Nay, Humphrey, if I be patient, you muſt be ſo too; this pleaſant conceited Gentleman hath wrought vpon my iudgement, and preuail'd: I pray you take care of your ſicke friend, Miſtreſſe Alice, and my good friends all—

sp.speaker: QVA.
sp.p: And no enormities.

sp.speaker: IVS.
sp.p: I inuite you home, with mee to my houſe, to ſupper: I will haue none feare to go along, for my intents are Ad correctionem, non ad deſtructionem; Ad aedificandum, non ad diruendum: ſo lead on.

sp.speaker: COK.
sp.p: Yes, and bring the Actors along, wee'll ha'the reſt o'the Play at home.

div.head: The EPILOGVE.
div.l: YOur Maieſty hath ſeene the Play, and you
can beſt allow it from your eare, and view.
You know the ſcope of Writers, and what ſtore,
of leaue is giuen them, if they take not more,
And turne it into licence: you can tell
if we haue vs'd that leaue you gaue vs, well:
Or whether wee to rage, or licence breake,
or be prophane, or make prophane men ſpeake?
This is your power to iudge (great Sir) and not
the enuy of a few. Which if wee haue got,
Wee value leſſe what their diſlike can bring,
if it ſo happy be, t' haue pleas'd the King.
